title.zh: 卷四十二 光武十王列傳
title.en: Volume 42: Biographies of Ten Princes of Guangwu
title.raw: 卷四十二 光武十王列傳 Volume 42: Biographies of Ten Princes of Guangwu

sentences.zh: 東海恭王彊

translations.literal: King Gong of East Sea, Qiang.
translations.idiomatic: King Gong of the East Sea, Liu Qiang

sentences.zh: 臣蒙恩得備蕃輔，特受二國，宮室禮樂，事事殊異，巍巍無量，訖無報稱。

translations.literal: Your servant receives grace and was able to serve as feudal assistant; specially received two states; palace chambers and ritual music—each affair peculiarly different; lofty without measure; to the end had no repayment to match.
translations.idiomatic: By your grace I was made a feudal bulwark and granted two kingdoms—palaces, ritual, music: everything beyond ordinary splendor. I have never matched such favor.

sentences.zh: 而自修不謹，連年被疾，為朝廷憂念。

translations.literal: Yet in self-cultivation I was not careful; year after year I have been ill, causing worry for the court.
translations.idiomatic: Yet I failed to keep my health; illness has plagued me year after year and burdened the court with worry.

sentences.zh: 皇太后、陛下哀憐臣強，感動發中，數遣使者太醫令丞方伎道術，絡驛不絕。

translations.literal: The empress dowager and Your Majesty pity your servant Qiang; moved from within the heart; repeatedly dispatched envoys, imperial physicians and assistants, recipe masters and arts of the Way—relay upon relay without cease.
translations.idiomatic: The empress dowager and Your Majesty have pitied me from the heart, dispatching envoys, palace physicians, and healers of every sort without cease.

sentences.zh: 臣伏惟厚恩，不知所言。

translations.literal: Your servant humbly considers the thick grace and does not know what to say.
translations.idiomatic: Your mercy leaves me speechless.

sentences.zh: 臣內自省視，氣力羸劣，日夜浸困，[3]終不復望見闕庭，奉承帷幄，孤負重恩，銜恨黃泉。

translations.literal: Your servant within examines himself: vital force weak and inferior; day and night gradually exhausted; [3] in the end cannot again hope to see the palace gates or attend the tent curtain; betraying heavy grace; holding regret in the yellow springs.
translations.idiomatic: I grow weaker daily and shall never again attend court under your canopy. I die owing you everything—a grief I carry to the grave.

sentences.zh: [4]身既夭命孤弱，復為皇太后、陛下憂慮，誠悲誠籩。

translations.literal: [4] My person is already by heaven's fate orphaned and weak, yet I again bring worry to the empress dowager and Your Majesty—I am truly grieved and truly anxious.
translations.idiomatic: [4] Heaven left me no heir, only frailty, yet I add to the empress dowager's and Your Majesty's cares—it grieves and terrifies me.

sentences.zh: 息政，小人也，猥當襲臣後，必非所以全利之也。

translations.literal: Son Zheng is a petty man; undeservedly would succeed your servant's posterity—surely not the means to wholly benefit him.
translations.idiomatic: My son Zheng is untested; he ought not inherit my title—it would not serve him or the state.

sentences.zh: 誠願還東海郡。

translations.literal: Truly I wish to return the East Sea commandery.
translations.idiomatic: I beg to surrender the kingdom of the East Sea.

sentences.zh: 天恩愍哀，以臣無男之故，[5]處臣三女小國侯，[6]此臣宿昔常計。

translations.literal: Heaven's grace pities; because your servant has no sons, [5] place your servant's three daughters as marquises of small states, [6] this your servant long planned.
translations.idiomatic: If heaven would pity me—having no sons—grant my three daughters small marquisates; I have long wished for this.

sentences.zh: [7]今天下新罹大憂，[8]惟陛下加供養皇太后，數進御餐。

translations.literal: [7] Now all-under-heaven newly suffers great grief; [8] only wish Your Majesty increase nourishing the empress dowager; frequently advance imperial meals.
translations.idiomatic: [7] The empire is newly struck by bereavement; [8] nourish the empress dowager yourself and see that she eats.

sentences.zh: 臣強困劣，言不能盡意。

translations.literal: Your servant Qiang is trapped and inferior; words cannot exhaust intent.
translations.idiomatic: I am too weak to say all I feel.

sentences.zh: 願並謝諸王，不意永不復相見也。

translations.literal: I wish together to thank the various kings; I did not expect never again to see each other.
translations.idiomatic: Give my farewell to my royal brothers—I never thought we would not meet again.

sentences.zh: 天子覽書悲慟，從太后出幸津門亭發哀。

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven read the letter and grieved greatly; following the empress dowager went out to favor Jinmen Pavilion and expressed grief.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor read the memorial and wept; he accompanied the empress dowager to Jinmen Pavilion to mourn.

translations.literal: [9] Ordered.
translations.idiomatic: Manuscript note nine: ordered—the clause continues with the minister of works.

translations.literal: (Grand)
translations.idiomatic: Variant gloss grand; some editions prefix that word to the minister of works in the next clause.

sentences.zh: *司空持節護喪事，大鴻臚副，宗正、將作大匠視喪事，贈以殊禮，升龍、旄頭、鸞輅、龍旗、虎賁百人。

translations.literal: Minister of Works holding credentials supervised funeral affairs; Grand Herald as deputy; Minister of the Imperial Clan and Court Architect inspected funeral affairs; gifted with special ritual—rising dragon, yak-tail pennants, phoenix carriage, dragon banners, one hundred tiger guards.
translations.idiomatic: The minister of works bore imperial credentials and directed the funeral, assisted by the grand herald; the minister of the imperial clan and the court architect oversaw rites. The gifts included dragon banners, yak-tail standards, the phoenix carriage, dragon flags, and a hundred imperial guards.

sentences.zh: [10]詔楚王英、趙王栩、北海王興、館陶公主、比陽公主及京師親戚四姓夫人、小侯皆會葬。

translations.literal: [10] Edict: King Ying of Chu, King Xu of Zhao, King Xing of Beihai, Princess Guantao, Princess Biyang, and capital-area kin, four-surname ladies, minor marquises—all assembled at burial.
translations.idiomatic: [10] The emperor summoned King Ying of Chu, King Xu of Zhao, King Xing of Beihai, the princesses of Guantao and Biyang, kin at court, ladies of the great houses, and lesser nobles to attend the funeral.

sentences.zh: [11]帝追惟彊深執謙儉，不欲厚葬以違其意，於是特詔中常侍杜岑及東海傅相曰：「王恭謙好禮，以德自終，遣送之物，務從約省，衣足斂形，茅車瓦器，物減於制，以彰王卓爾獨行之志。

translations.literal: [11] The emperor recalled Qiang's deep holding of humility and frugality; did not wish lavish burial to violate his intent; therefore specially edicted attendant Du Cen and East Sea tutor and chancellor, saying: "The king was respectful, humble, and loved ritual; ended himself by virtue; objects for sending off—strive to follow reduction and economy; garments enough to wrap the form; thatched cart, earthen vessels; goods less than regulation—to manifest the king's outstanding solitary practice of intent."
translations.idiomatic: [11] Remembering Qiang's humility, the emperor would not bury him richly against his wishes. He told Du Cen and the East Sea officials: "The king lived modestly. Keep the grave goods spare—only enough to cover the body, a straw cart and clay vessels, below sumptuary rank—to honor his singular integrity."

sentences.zh: [12]將作大匠留起陵廟。」

translations.literal: [12] Court Architect Liu raised mound-temple."
translations.idiomatic: [12] Let the court architect raise his tomb-shrine."

sentences.zh: 立四十四年薨，子頃王肅嗣。

translations.literal: Established forty-four years and died; son King Qing Su succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: He ruled forty-four years; his son King Qing Su succeeded.

sentences.zh: 永元十六年，封肅弟二十一人皆為列侯。

translations.literal: In the sixteenth year of Yongyuan, Su's twenty-one younger brothers were all enfeoffed as full marquises.
translations.idiomatic: In Yongyuan 16 twenty-one younger brothers of Su were made full marquises.

sentences.zh: 肅性謙儉，循恭王法度。

translations.literal: Su's nature was humble and frugal; followed King Gong's norms.
translations.idiomatic: Su was modest like his father and kept to his father's standards.

sentences.zh: 永初中，以西羌未平，上錢二千萬。

translations.literal: In the Yongchu period, because Western Qiang were not pacified, he submitted cash twenty million.
translations.idiomatic: During Yongchu he sent twenty million cash to help against the Western Qiang.

sentences.zh: 元初中，復上縑萬匹，以助國費，鄧太后下詔襃納焉。

translations.literal: In the Yuanchu period, again submitted ten thousand bolts of silk to aid state expenses; Empress Dowager Deng issued edict praising and accepting.
translations.idiomatic: In Yuanchu he gave ten thousand bolts of silk for state expenses; Empress Dowager Deng praised him and accepted the gift.

sentences.zh: 立二十三年薨，子孝王臻嗣。

translations.literal: Established twenty-three years and died; son King Xiao Zhen succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: After twenty-three years he died; King Xiao Zhen succeeded.

sentences.zh: 永建二年，封臻二弟敏、儉為鄉侯。

translations.literal: In the second year of Yongjian, Zhen's two younger brothers Min and Jian were enfeoffed as village marquises.
translations.idiomatic: In Yongjian 2 Min and Jian, Zhen's brothers, became village marquises.

sentences.zh: 臻及弟蒸鄉侯儉並有篤行，母卒，皆吐血毀眥。

translations.literal: Zhen together with younger brother Village Marquis Jian of Zheng township both had devoted conduct; when mother died, both spat blood and ruined eye corners.
translations.idiomatic: Zhen and his brother Jian, marquis of Zheng village, mourned deeply—both spat blood and wasted away until their eyes sank.

sentences.zh: [13]至服練紅，兄弟追念初喪父，幼小，哀禮有闕，因復重行喪制。

translations.literal: [13] When wearing refined red, the brothers recalled first losing father—young and small—mourning ritual had gaps; therefore again practiced mourning regulations.
translations.idiomatic: [13] After the second mourning period they remembered how young they had been when their father died and had failed part of the rites—they observed mourning again.

sentences.zh: [14]臻性敦厚有恩，常分租秩賑給諸父昆弟。

translations.literal: [14] Zhen's nature was honest and thick, with kindness; constantly divided rent-rank to relieve uncles and brothers.
translations.idiomatic: [14] Zhen was generous and often shared his income with uncles and brothers.

sentences.zh: 國相籍襃具以狀聞，順帝美之，制詔大將軍、三公、大鴻臚曰：「東海王臻以近蕃之尊，少襲王爵，膺受多福，未知艱難，而能克己率禮，孝敬自然，事親盡愛，送終竭哀，降儀從士，寢苫三年。

translations.literal: Chancellor Ji Bao fully reported the situation on hearing; Emperor Shun praised it; issued edict to grand general, three dukes, grand herald, saying: "East Sea King Zhen—by near feudatory's dignity—young inherited king rank; chest receives much fortune; did not know hardship; yet able to restrain self and lead ritual; filial respect natural; serving kin exhausted love; sending end exhausted grief; lowered rites following scholars; slept on straw three years."
translations.idiomatic: Chancellor Ji Bao reported all this; Emperor Shun issued an edict to the general-in-chief, the three dukes, and the grand herald: "Though young when he inherited, King Zhen of the East Sea has embraced hardship, disciplined himself, honored his mother with perfect filial love, mourned her to the utmost, humbled himself like a scholar, and slept on coarse rush for three years."

sentences.zh: [15]和睦兄弟，恤養孤弱，至孝純備，仁義兼弘，朕甚嘉焉。

translations.literal: [15] Harmonized brothers; pitied and raised orphaned weak—utmost filial purity complete; humanity and righteousness together grand—I greatly praise this.
translations.idiomatic: [15] He lives at peace with his brothers and cares for the helpless—filial piety and humane duty united. I applaud him."

sentences.zh: 夫勸善厲俗，為國所先。

translations.literal: As for urging goodness and sharpening custom—that is what the state puts first.
translations.idiomatic: Encouraging virtue and customs is the state's first task."

sentences.zh: 曩者東平孝王敞兄弟行孝，喪母如禮，有增戶之封。

translations.literal: Formerly East Peace Filial King Chang's brothers practiced filiality; buried mother according to ritual—had increased-household enfeoffment.
translations.idiomatic: When King Chang of East Peace and his brothers mourned their mother by the book, the court added households to their fiefs."

sentences.zh: 詩云：『永世克孝，念茲皇祖。

translations.literal: The Poetry says: 'Through ages able filial—think on this august ancestor.
translations.idiomatic: The Poetry says, 'Forever filial—remember the royal ancestors."

sentences.zh: 』[16]今增臻封五千戶，儉五百戶，光啟土宇，以酬厥德。」

translations.literal: ' [16] Now increase Zhen's fief five thousand households; Jian five hundred households—broadly open soil and domain—to repay this virtue."
translations.idiomatic: [16] Add five thousand households to Zhen's fief and five hundred to Jian—expand their lands as their reward."

sentences.zh: 立三十一年薨，子懿王祗嗣。

translations.literal: Established thirty-one years and died; son King Yi Zhi succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: After thirty-one years he died; King Yi Zhi succeeded.

sentences.zh: 初平四年，遣子琬至長安奉章，獻帝封琬汶陽侯，拜為平原相。

translations.literal: In the fourth year of Chuping, sent son Wan to Chang'an presenting memorial; Emperor Xian enfeoffed Wan Marquis of Wenyang, appointed plain chancellor.
translations.idiomatic: In Chuping 4 he sent his son Wan to Chang'an with a memorial; Emperor Xian made Wan marquis of Wenyang and appointed him chancellor of Pingyuan.

sentences.zh: 祗立四十四年薨，子羨嗣。

translations.literal: Zhi established forty-four years and died; son Xian succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: Zhi ruled forty-four years; Xian succeeded.

sentences.zh: 二十年，魏受禪，以為崇德侯。

translations.literal: In the twentieth year, Wei received abdication; he was made Marquis of Honoring Virtue.
translations.idiomatic: In the twentieth year the Wei accepted the abdication and named him marquis of Chongde.

sentences.zh: 沛獻王輔

translations.literal: King Xian of Pei, Fu.
translations.idiomatic: King Xian of Pei, Liu Fu

sentences.zh: 沛獻王輔，建武十五年封右*

translations.literal: King Xian of Pei, Fu—in the fifteenth year of Jianwu was enfeoffed as Right —
translations.idiomatic: King Xian of Pei, Liu Fu—in Jianwu 15 was enfeoffed duke of Right—

translations.literal: (Feng)
translations.idiomatic: The Feng syllable for Right Fengyi duke.

sentences.zh: *翊公。

translations.literal: —Yi duke.
translations.idiomatic: —Fengyi.

sentences.zh: 十七年，郭后廢為中山太后，故徙輔為中山王，並食常山郡。

translations.literal: In the seventeenth year, Empress Guo was deposed as Zhongshan empress dowager; therefore Fu was transferred to be Zhongshan king, together eating Changshan commandery.
translations.idiomatic: In Jianwu 17 Empress Guo was demoted to dowager of Zhongshan; Fu became king of Zhongshan with Changshan added to his income.

sentences.zh: 二十年，復徙封沛王。

translations.literal: In the twentieth year, again transferred enfeoffment to Pei king.
translations.idiomatic: In Jianwu 20 he was transferred back as king of Pei.

sentences.zh: 時禁網尚疏，諸王皆在京師，競修名譽，爭禮四方賓客。

translations.literal: At that time prohibition nets were still loose; various kings were all in the capital; vied to cultivate fame; competed to honor four-directions guests.
translations.idiomatic: While regulations were still loose the kings lived in the capital, vying for renown and courting clients from every quarter.

sentences.zh: 壽光侯劉鯉，更始子也，得幸於輔。

translations.literal: Marquis of Shouguang Liu Li, Gengshi's son, obtained favor from Fu.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Li of Shouguang—Emperor Gengshi's son—was a favorite of Fu.

sentences.zh: 鯉怨劉盆子害其父，因輔結客，報殺盆子兄故式侯恭，輔坐系詔獄，三日乃得出。

translations.literal: Li resented Liu Penzi harming his father; through Fu associated with guests; in revenge killed Penzi's elder brother former Marquis Gong of Shi; Fu sat guilty and was bound in imperial prison—three days then could emerge.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Li blamed Liu Penzi for his father's death and through Fu gathered retainers to murder Penzi's brother, the former Marquis Gong. Fu was jailed in the imperial prison for three days before release.

sentences.zh: 自是後，諸王賓客多坐刑罰，各循法度。

translations.literal: From this after, various kings' guests many sat punishments; each followed law and measure.
translations.idiomatic: After that clients of the kings often faced prosecution; everyone watched the law more closely.

sentences.zh: 二十八年，就國。

translations.literal: In the twenty-eighth year, went to state.
translations.idiomatic: In Jianwu 28 he went to his fief.

sentences.zh: 中元二年，封輔子寶為沛侯。

translations.literal: In the second year of Zhongyuan, Fu's son Bao was enfeoffed Marquis of Pei.
translations.idiomatic: In Zhongyuan 2 his son Bao became marquis of Pei.

sentences.zh: 永平元年，封寶弟嘉為僮侯。

translations.literal: In the first year of Yongping, Bao's younger brother Jia was enfeoffed Marquis of Tong.
translations.idiomatic: In Yongping 1 Bao's brother Jia became marquis of Tong.

translations.literal: Editorial mark seventeen; line break or variant in the received text.
translations.idiomatic: Editorial note seventeen in the source.

sentences.zh: 輔矜嚴有法度，好經書，善說京氏易、孝經、論語傳及圖讖，作五經論，時號之曰沛王通論。

translations.literal: Fu was stern and dignified with law and measure; loved classics; good at explaining Jing lineage Changes, Classic of Filial Piety, Analects commentary, and chart-weft prophecies; composed Five Classics Discourse—at the time titled it Pei King's Comprehensive Discourse.
translations.idiomatic: Fu was austere and disciplined, loved the classics, lectured on the Jing school Changes, the Filial Classic, Analects commentary, and prognostic texts, and wrote the Discourse on the Five Classics—called the Pei King's Comprehensive Discourse.

sentences.zh: 在國謹節，終始如一，稱為賢王。

translations.literal: In the state careful in restraint; end and beginning like one—called worthy king.
translations.idiomatic: In his kingdom he was prudent from first to last and was known as a worthy prince.

sentences.zh: 顯宗敬重，數加賞賜。

translations.literal: Emperor Ming respected and honored; repeatedly increased gifts and grants.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Ming esteemed him and rewarded him often.

sentences.zh: 立四十六年薨，子釐王定嗣。

translations.literal: Established forty-six years and died; son King Li Ding succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: He ruled forty-six years; King Li Ding succeeded.

sentences.zh: [18]元和二年，封定弟十二人為鄉侯。

translations.literal: [18] In the second year of Yuanhe, Ding's twelve younger brothers were enfeoffed as village marquises.
translations.idiomatic: [18] In Yuanhe 2 twelve of Ding's brothers became village marquises.

sentences.zh: 定立十一年薨，子節王正嗣。

translations.literal: Ding established eleven years and died; son King Jie Zheng succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: Ding ruled eleven years; King Jie Zheng succeeded.

sentences.zh: 元興元年，封正弟二人為縣侯。

translations.literal: In the first year of Yuanxing, Zheng's two younger brothers were enfeoffed as county marquises.
translations.idiomatic: In Yuanxing 1 two of Zheng's brothers became county marquises.

sentences.zh: 正立十四年薨，子孝王廣嗣。

translations.literal: Zheng established fourteen years and died; son King Xiao Guang succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: Zheng ruled fourteen years; King Xiao Guang succeeded.

sentences.zh: 有固疾。

translations.literal: Had chronic illness.
translations.idiomatic: He suffered chronic illness.

sentences.zh: 安帝詔廣祖母周領王家事。

translations.literal: Emperor An edicted Guang's grandmother Zhou to lead royal household affairs.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor An ordered Guang's grandmother Lady Zhou to administer the royal household.

sentences.zh: 周明正有法禮，漢安中薨，順帝下詔曰：「沛王祖母太夫人周，秉心淑慎，導王以仁，使光祿大夫贈以妃印綬。」

translations.literal: Zhou was bright and upright with law and ritual; in Han'an died; Emperor Shun issued edict saying: "Pei king's grandmother Lady Zhou—holds heart virtuous and careful; guided king by humanity; ordered grand counselor of the palace present her with consort seals and ribbons."
translations.idiomatic: Lady Zhou was principled and died in the Han'an era. Emperor Shun said: "The king of Pei's grandmother Lady Zhou guided him with kindness—send the grand counselor of the palace to honor her with a consort's seal."

sentences.zh: 廣立三十五年薨，子幽王榮嗣。

translations.literal: Guang established thirty-five years and died; son King You Rong succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: Guang ruled thirty-five years; King You Rong succeeded.

sentences.zh: 立二十年薨，子孝王琮嗣。

translations.literal: Established twenty years and died; son King Xiao Cong succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: After twenty years he died; King Xiao Cong succeeded.

sentences.zh: 薨，子恭王曜嗣。

translations.literal: Died; son King Gong Yao succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: He died; King Gong Yao succeeded.

sentences.zh: 薨，子契嗣；

translations.literal: Died; son Qi succeeded;
translations.idiomatic: He died; Qi succeeded;

sentences.zh: 魏受禪，以為崇德侯。

translations.literal: Wei received abdication; he was made Marquis of Honoring Virtue.
translations.idiomatic: When Wei received the abdication he was named marquis of Chongde.

sentences.zh: 楚王英

translations.literal: King Ying of Chu.
translations.idiomatic: King Ying of Chu

sentences.zh: 英少時好遊俠，交通賓客，晚節更喜黃老，學為浮屠齋戒祭祀。

translations.literal: Ying in youth loved roaming knights-errant; associated with guests; in later years further delighted in Huang-Lao; studied and practiced Buddha fasting and sacrifice.
translations.idiomatic: In youth Ying ran with swordsmen and kept clients; later he turned to Huang-Lao and Buddhist fasting and sacrifice.

sentences.zh: [21]八年，詔令天下死罪皆入縑贖。

translations.literal: [21] In the eighth year, edict ordered all-under-heaven capital crimes all enter silk to redeem.
translations.idiomatic: [21] In the eighth year an edict allowed capital crimes throughout the realm to be redeemed with silk.

sentences.zh: 英遣郎中令奉黃縑白紈三十匹詣國相曰：「托在蕃輔，過惡累積，歡喜大恩，奉送縑帛，以贖愆罪。

translations.literal: Ying sent chief of gentlemen presenting thirty bolts yellow silk and white fine silk to the state chancellor, saying: "Relying on being feudal assistant, faults and evils accumulated; rejoice in great grace; offering silk and cloth to redeem fault-crimes."
translations.idiomatic: Ying sent his chief of gentlemen with thirty bolts of silk to his chancellor: "As a feudal prince I have piled up offenses; I rejoice in this mercy and send silk to redeem my guilt."

sentences.zh: 」國相以聞。

translations.literal: " The state chancellor reported it on hearing.
translations.idiomatic: The chancellor forwarded the letter to the throne.

sentences.zh: 詔報曰：「楚王誦黃老之微言，尚浮屠之仁祠，絜齋三月，與神為誓，何嫌何疑，當有悔吝？

translations.literal: Edict replied, saying: "King Ying recites Huang-Lao's subtle words; esteems Buddha's humane shrine; pure fasted three months; swore covenant with spirits—what suspicion, what doubt—should there be regret or stinginess?
translations.idiomatic: The reply read: "The king of Chu studies Huang-Lao, honors the Buddha, fasted three months, and swore vows to the gods—what crime needs redeeming? Keep your silk."

sentences.zh: 其還贖，以助伊蒲塞桑門之盛饌。

translations.literal: Return the redemption; to assist upāsaka śramaṇa grand feast.
translations.idiomatic: Return it to feast the lay Buddhists and monks."

sentences.zh: 」[22]因以班示諸國中傅。

translations.literal: " [22] Thereupon distributed and displayed to various states' tutors.
translations.idiomatic: [22] The edict was copied to every kingdom's tutor."

sentences.zh: 英後遂大交通方士，作金龜玉鶴，刻文字以為符瑞。

translations.literal: Ying afterward then greatly associated with recipe masters; made gold tortoise and jade crane; carved writing to make talisman omens.
translations.idiomatic: Ying then patronized occult masters, cast golden tortoises and jade cranes, and carved prophetic inscriptions.

sentences.zh: 十三年，男子燕廣告英與漁陽王平、顏忠等造作圖書，有逆謀，事下案驗。

translations.literal: In the thirteenth year, man Yan Guang accused Ying with Yuyang Wang Ping, Yan Zhong, and others making charts and texts—had rebellious plot; affair descended for case verification.
translations.idiomatic: In year 13 Yan Guang charged Ying with plotting rebellion through charts and texts with Wang Ping of Yuyang and Yan Zhong; the case was investigated.

sentences.zh: 有司奏英招聚姦猾，造作圖讖，擅相官秩，置諸侯王公將軍二千石，大逆不道，請誅之。

translations.literal: Authorities memorialized Ying gathered crafty and cunning; made chart-weft prophecies; presumptuously assigned each other offices and ranks; set up feudal lords, kings, dukes, generals, two thousand piculs—great rebellion not following the Way—requested execution.
translations.idiomatic: The ministry accused Ying of conspiracy, forging prognostications, naming officials and generals without authority—the gravest treason—and asked for his death.

sentences.zh: 帝以親親不忍，乃廢英，徙丹陽涇縣，[23]賜湯沐邑五百戶。

translations.literal: The emperor by loving kin could not bear; then deposed Ying, relocated Danyang Jing county, [23] granted bath-and-grant fief five hundred households.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor spared his kinsman: he stripped Ying's title and moved him to Jing in Danyang [23] with five hundred households for income.

sentences.zh: [24]遣大鴻臚持節護送，使伎人奴婢*

translations.literal: [24] Sent grand herald holding credentials escorting; ordered entertainers and slave servants —
translations.idiomatic: [24] The grand herald escorted him with credentials—performers and servants—

sentences.zh: (妓士)

translations.literal: (female performers)
translations.idiomatic: Alternate gloss female performers or musicians for the fragment joined to the previous clause.

sentences.zh: **〔工技〕*鼓吹悉從，得乘輜軿，[25]持兵弩，行道射獵，極意自娛。

translations.literal: ** [craftsmen] * drum-and-pipe all followed; could ride covered wagons, [25] hold weapons and crossbows; on road shoot and hunt; exhaust intent self-amusing.
translations.idiomatic: Craftsmen, musicians, and guards followed him; he rode in a covered carriage note twenty-five in the source, carried arms, hunted along the road, and amused himself freely.

sentences.zh: 男女為侯主者，食邑如故。

translations.literal: Men and women who were marquises or lords—food fiefs as before.
translations.idiomatic: Children who held noble rank kept their fiefs.

sentences.zh: 楚太后勿上璽綬，留住楚宮。

translations.literal: Chu empress dowager do not ascend seals and ribbons; stay and remain Chu palace.
translations.idiomatic: The dowager of Chu was not to surrender her seal; she stayed in the Chu palace.

sentences.zh: 明年，英至丹陽，自殺。

translations.literal: Next year, Ying reached Danyang; killed himself.
translations.idiomatic: The next year Ying reached Danyang and took his own life.

sentences.zh: 立三十三年，國除。

translations.literal: Established thirty-three years; state abolished.
translations.idiomatic: After thirty-three years the kingdom was abolished.

sentences.zh: 詔遣光祿大夫持節吊祠，贈賵如法，加賜列侯印綬，以諸侯禮葬於涇。

translations.literal: Edict dispatched grand counselor of the palace holding credentials to condole and sacrifice; burial gifts according to law; additionally granted full marquis seals and ribbons; buried by feudal lords ritual at Jing.
translations.idiomatic: A grand counselor of the palace was sent to mourn and sacrifice; burial gifts followed statute; he was given a full marquis's seals and buried at Jing with princely honors.

sentences.zh: 遣中黃門占護其妻子。

translations.literal: Sent central yellow gate to oversee and protect his wife and children.
translations.idiomatic: Eunuchs of the palace were sent to watch over his family.

sentences.zh: [26]悉出楚官屬無辭語者。

translations.literal: [26] All released Chu officials and staff without incriminating statements.
translations.idiomatic: [26] Every Chu official not named in the indictments was released.

sentences.zh: 制詔許太后曰：「國家始聞楚事，幸其不然。

translations.literal: Imperial edict to Empress Dowager Xu, saying: "The state first heard Chu affair; hoped it was not so.
translations.idiomatic: An edict to Empress Dowager Xu said: "When we first heard of Chu we prayed it was false."

sentences.zh: 既知審實，懷用悼灼，庶欲宥全王身，令保卒天年，而王不念顧太后，竟不自免。

translations.literal: Having learned verified truth, breast used grief burned; hoped to forgive and preserve whole the king's person; ordered protect and complete heaven's years; yet king did not think to care for empress dowager; in the end could not self-escape.
translations.idiomatic: When the truth came out our grief was bitter; we meant to spare the king and let him live out his years, yet he forgot you, madam, and could not save himself."

sentences.zh: 此天命也，無可柰何！

translations.literal: This is heaven's mandate—nothing can be done!
translations.idiomatic: That is heaven's will—nothing could be done."

sentences.zh: 太后其保養幼弱，勉強飲食。

translations.literal: Empress dowager—nourish and protect the young and weak; force yourself drink and eat.
translations.idiomatic: Madam, care for the children and force yourself to eat."

sentences.zh: 諸許願王富貴，人情也。

translations.literal: The various Xus wish king wealth and honor—human feeling.
translations.idiomatic: Your clan hoped the king would rise—such is only human."

sentences.zh: 已詔有司，出其有謀者，令安田宅。

translations.literal: Already edicted responsible offices: release those who had plotting—order secure fields and houses.
translations.idiomatic: I have ordered the innocent freed and their lands restored."

sentences.zh: 」於是封燕廣為折奸侯。

translations.literal: " Thereupon enfeoffed Yan Guang Marquis Who Breaks Treachery.
translations.idiomatic: Yan Guang was enfeoffed marquis of Zhajian.

sentences.zh: 楚獄遂至累年，其辭語相連，自京師親戚諸侯州郡豪桀及考案吏，阿附相陷，坐死徙者以千數。

translations.literal: Chu prison then reached piled years; their statements and words mutually linked— from capital-area kin, feudal lords, province commandery heroes, and examining clerks—currying and attaching mutually trapped—sat death and exile counted by thousands.
translations.idiomatic: The Chu case dragged on for years; confessions implicated kin at court, princes, local magnates, and investigators who curried favor—thousands died or were banished.

sentences.zh: 種後徙封六侯。

translations.literal: Zhong afterward was transferred enfeoffment to six marquises.
translations.idiomatic: Zhong's line was later reassigned among six secondary marquisates.

sentences.zh: [28]卒，子度嗣。

translations.literal: [28] Died; son Du succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: He died note twenty-eight in the source; his son Du inherited.

sentences.zh: 度卒，子拘嗣，傳國於後。

translations.literal: Du died; son Ju succeeded; transmitted the state to posterity.
translations.idiomatic: When Du died, Ju inherited; the title passed down thereafter.

sentences.zh: 濟南安王康

translations.literal: King Kang of Jinan.
translations.idiomatic: King Kang of Jinan

sentences.zh: 濟南安王康，建武十五年封濟南公，十七年進爵為王，二十八年就國。

translations.literal: King Kang of Jinan—in the fifteenth year of Jianwu was enfeoffed Jinan duke; in the seventeenth year advanced rank to king; in the twenty-eighth year went to state.
translations.idiomatic: King Kang of Jinan was made duke of Jinan in Jianwu 15, raised to king in Jianwu 17, and went to his fief in Jianwu 28.

sentences.zh: 三十年，以平原之祝阿、安德、朝陽、平昌、隰陰、重丘六縣益濟南國。

translations.literal: In the thirtieth year, taking Pingyuan's Zhu'e, Ande, Chaoyang, Pingchang, Xiyin, Chongqiu six counties to augment Jinan state.
translations.idiomatic: In Jianwu 30 six counties from Pingyuan—Zhu'e, Ande, Chaoyang, Pingchang, Xiyin, and Chongqiu—were added to Jinan.

sentences.zh: 中元二年，封康子德為東武城侯。

translations.literal: In the second year of Zhongyuan, Kang's son De was enfeoffed Marquis of Eastern Wucheng.
translations.idiomatic: In Zhongyuan 2 Kang's son De became marquis of Dongwucheng.

translations.literal: Editorial mark twenty-nine in the received text.
translations.idiomatic: Editorial note twenty-nine in the source.

sentences.zh: 康在國不循法度，交通賓客。

translations.literal: Kang in the state did not follow law and measure; associated with guests.
translations.idiomatic: In his kingdom Kang ignored regulations and kept company with clients.

sentences.zh: 其後，人上書告康招來州郡姦猾漁陽顏忠、劉子產等，又多遺其繒帛，案圖書，謀議不軌。

translations.literal: Afterward people submitted memorials accusing Kang of summoning province commandery crafty men Yuyang Yan Zhong, Liu Zichan, and others; also repeatedly gifting them silks; examining charts and texts; plotting beyond the track.
translations.idiomatic: Later informers charged Kang with recruiting scoundrels such as Yan Zhong and Liu Zichan of Yuyang, sending them silk, studying prognostications, and plotting sedition.

sentences.zh: 事下考，有司舉奏之，顯宗以親親故，不忍窮竟其事，但削祝阿、隰陰、東朝陽、安德、西平昌五縣。

translations.literal: Affair descended for examination; authorities memorialized; Emperor Ming by loving-kin reason could not bear exhaustively finishing the affair—only stripped Zhu'e, Xiyin, Eastern Chaoyang, Ande, Western Pingchang five counties.
translations.idiomatic: The case was investigated and officials memorialized. Emperor Ming spared his kinsman a full trial and merely stripped five counties from the fief.

translations.literal: Editorial mark thirty in the received text.
translations.idiomatic: Editorial note thirty in the source.

sentences.zh: 建初八年，肅宗復還所削地，康遂多殖財貨，大修宮室，奴婢至千四百人，廄馬千二百匹，私田八百頃，奢侈恣欲，遊觀無節。

translations.literal: In the eighth year of Jianchu, Emperor Zhang again returned what was stripped land; Kang then greatly multiplied wealth goods, greatly built palace chambers—slave servants reached fourteen hundred; stable horses twelve hundred; private fields eight hundred qing—luxury and indulgence without restraint; roaming and viewing without measure.
translations.idiomatic: In Jianchu 8 Emperor Zhang restored the lost territory. Kang then piled up riches, threw up halls, kept fourteen hundred servants and twelve hundred horses, seized eight hundred qing of private fields, and lived without restraint.

sentences.zh: 永元初，國傅何敞上疏諫康曰：「蓋聞諸侯之義，制節謹度，然後能保其社稷，和其民人。

translations.literal: At the beginning of Yongyuan, state tutor He Chang submitted memorial remonstrating Kang, saying: "I have heard the feudal lords' meaning: regulate restraint and careful measure—then able to preserve their altars of soil and grain and harmonize their people.
translations.idiomatic: Early in Yongyuan the tutor He Chang warned Kang: "The feudal code demands moderation—only then can a prince save his altars and keep peace among his people."

sentences.zh: [31]大王以骨肉之親，享食茅土，當施張政令，明其典法，出入進止，宜有期度，輿馬臺隸，應為科品。

translations.literal: [31] Great king by flesh-and-bone kinship enjoys eating with hemp soil—ought to spread government orders, clarify statutes; going out and entering, advancing and stopping—ought to have scheduled measure; carriages, horses, terraces, and clerks—ought to match graded ranks.
translations.idiomatic: [31] As Your Highness is kin of the blood and holds a fief, you should publish clear laws and keep every outing, equipage, and staff within proper rank."

sentences.zh: [32]而今奴婢廄馬皆有千餘，增無用之口，以自蠶食。

translations.literal: [32] Yet now slave servants and stable horses both have over a thousand—increasing useless mouths to devour yourself.
translations.idiomatic: [32] Yet you feed over a thousand slaves and horses—useless mouths that drain your house."

sentences.zh: [33]宮婢閉隔，失其天性，惑亂和氣。

translations.literal: [33] Palace maids shut apart—lose their heaven nature; confuse and disorder harmonious qi.
translations.idiomatic: [33] Women locked in the inner quarters lose their natural lives and disturb the moral climate."

sentences.zh: 又多起內第，觸犯防禁，費以巨萬，[34]而功猶未半。

translations.literal: [34] Also many raising inner mansions—touch and violate ward prohibitions—expense by tens of thousands, [34] yet achievement still not half.
translations.idiomatic: [34] You raise courtyard after courtyard against the statutes and spend fortunes note thirty-four in the source—yet the work is barely half done."

sentences.zh: 夫文繁者質荒，木勝者人亡，[35]皆非所以奉禮承上，傳福無窮者也。

translations.literal: When pattern is excessive substance wastes; when wood prevails people perish—[35] neither is means to uphold ritual and receive superiors or transmit blessing without end.
translations.idiomatic: Ornament without substance ruins a house; timber that overwhelms its foundation kills it note thirty-five in the source—neither serves ritual or lasting fortune."

sentences.zh: 故楚作章華以凶，[36]吳興姑蘇而滅，[37]景公千駟，民無稱焉。

translations.literal: Therefore Chu built Zhanghua thereby ill; [36] Wu raised Gusu thereby destroyed; [37] Duke Jing thousand teams of four—people had nothing to praise.
translations.idiomatic: Chu raised Zhanghua and fell note thirty-six in the source; Wu built Gusu and perished note thirty-seven in the source; Duke Jing's thousand four-horse teams earned him no praise."

sentences.zh: [38]今數遊諸第，晨夜無節，又非所以遠防未然，臨深履薄之法也。

translations.literal: [38] Now repeatedly roaming various mansions—morning and night without measure—is again not the means to far guard before it happens—approaching deep treading thin method.
translations.idiomatic: [38] Roaming your villas day and night is no way to rule as though on a cliff's edge."

sentences.zh: 願大王修恭儉，遵古制，省奴婢之口，減乘馬之數，斥私田之富，節遊觀之宴，以禮起居，則敞乃敢安心自保。

translations.literal: I wish great king cultivate respectful frugality; follow ancient regulations; reduce slave mouths; decrease riding horses' number; dismiss private fields' wealth; moderate roaming-viewing feasts; by ritual rising and resting—then Chang dares settle mind self-preserving.
translations.idiomatic: Cut retainers and horses, sell surplus land, temper your outings, live by the rites—then I dare rest easy."

sentences.zh: 惟大王深慮愚言。

translations.literal: Only great king deeply consider foolish words.
translations.idiomatic: Think deeply on these plain words."

sentences.zh: 」康素敬重敞，雖無所嫌啎，然終不能改。

translations.literal: " Kang always respected Chang; although nothing mutually obstructed—yet in the end could not change.
translations.idiomatic: Kang respected He Chang but never changed his ways.

sentences.zh: 錯立六年薨，子孝王香嗣。

translations.literal: Cuo established six years and died; son King Xiao Xiang succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: King Cuo ruled six years; King Xiao Xiang succeeded.

sentences.zh: 永初二年，封香弟四人為列侯。

translations.literal: In the second year of Yongchu, Xiang's four younger brothers were enfeoffed as full marquises.
translations.idiomatic: In Yongchu 2 four of Xiang's brothers became full marquises.

sentences.zh: 香篤行，好經書。

translations.literal: Xiang had devoted conduct; loved classics.
translations.idiomatic: Xiang had a serious nature and loved the classics.

sentences.zh: 初，叔父篤有罪不得封，西平昌侯昱坐法失侯，香乃上書分爵土封篤子丸、昱子嵩，皆為列侯。

translations.literal: Earlier, uncle Du had guilt and could not be enfeoffed; Western Pingchang Marquis Yu sat by law and lost marquisate; Xiang then submitted memorial dividing noble soil to enfeoff Du's son Wan and Yu's son Song—both became full marquises.
translations.idiomatic: Earlier his uncle Du had been barred from a fief and Marquis Yu of Xipingchang lost his title for a crime; Xiang memorialized to carve land from his domain for Du's son Wan and Yu's Song—both became full marquises.

sentences.zh: 香立二十年薨，無子，國絕。

translations.literal: Xiang established twenty years and died; no son; state cut off.
translations.idiomatic: Xiang ruled twenty years and died without an heir; the kingdom lapsed.

sentences.zh: 永建元年，順帝立錯子阜陽侯顯為嗣，是為釐王。

translations.literal: In the first year of Yongjian, Emperor Shun established Cuo's son Fuyang Marquis Xian as heir—this is King Li.
translations.idiomatic: In Yongjian 1 Emperor Shun named Xian, marquis of Fuyang and son of Cuo, as heir—the future King Li.

sentences.zh: 立三年薨，子悼王廣嗣。

translations.literal: Established three years and died; son King Dao Guang succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: He ruled three years; King Dao Guang succeeded.

sentences.zh: 永建五年，封廣弟文為樂城亭侯。

translations.literal: In the fifth year of Yongjian, Guang's younger brother Wen was enfeoffed village marquis of Lecheng.
translations.idiomatic: In Yongjian 5 Guang's brother Wen became village marquis of Lecheng.

sentences.zh: 廣立二十五年，永興元年薨，無子，國除。

translations.literal: Guang established twenty-five years; in the first year of Yongxing died; no son; state abolished.
translations.idiomatic: Guang ruled twenty-five years and died in Yongxing 1 without an heir; the kingdom was abolished.

sentences.zh: 東平憲王蒼

translations.literal: King Xian of East Peace, Cang.
translations.idiomatic: King Xian of East Peace, Liu Cang

sentences.zh: 東平憲王蒼，建武十五年封東平公，十七年進爵為王。

translations.literal: King Xian of East Peace, Cang—in the fifteenth year of Jianwu was enfeoffed East Peace duke; in the seventeenth year advanced rank to king.
translations.idiomatic: King Xian of East Peace was made duke of East Peace in Jianwu 15 and king in Jianwu 17.

sentences.zh: 蒼少好經書，雅有智思，為人美鬚髯，要帶八圍，顯宗甚愛重之。

translations.literal: Cang in youth loved classics; elegantly had wisdom and thought; as person beautiful beard and whiskers; waist belt eight spans—Emperor Ming greatly loved and honored him.
translations.idiomatic: Cang loved the classics from youth and had a keen mind; he wore a full beard and an eight-span girdle—Emperor Ming treasured him.

sentences.zh: 及即位，拜為驃騎將軍，置長史掾史員四十人，位在三公上。

translations.literal: When he ascended the throne, appointed him general of agile cavalry; established chief clerk and clerk staff forty persons—rank above the three dukes.
translations.idiomatic: When Ming took the throne he named Cang general of agile cavalry with a forty-man staff—rank above the three dukes.

translations.literal: Editorial mark forty in the received text.
translations.idiomatic: Editorial note forty in the source.

sentences.zh: 永平元年，封蒼子二人為縣侯。

translations.literal: In the first year of Yongping, Cang's two sons were enfeoffed as county marquises.
translations.idiomatic: In Yongping 1 two of Cang's sons became county marquises.

sentences.zh: 二年，以東郡之壽張、須昌，山陽之南平陽、*

translations.literal: In the second year, taking Eastern Commandery's Shouzhang, Xuchang, Shanyang's Southern Pingyang —
translations.idiomatic: In Yongping 2 the counties Shouzhang and Xuchang from Dong commandery and Nanpingyang from Shanyang—

translations.literal: (Gao)
translations.idiomatic: Variant graph for the place-name syllable before Huling.

sentences.zh: **[橐]*、湖陵五縣益東平國。

translations.literal: ** [Tuo]*, Huling five counties augment East Peace state.
translations.idiomatic: —Huling in five counties added to the kingdom of East Peace.

sentences.zh: [41]是時中興三十餘年，四方無虞，蒼以天下化平，宜修禮樂，乃與公卿共議定南北郊冠冕車服制度，及光武廟登歌八佾舞數，語在禮樂、輿服志。

translations.literal: [41] At this time restoration thirty-some years; four directions without anxiety; Cang thought all-under-heaven transformed peaceful—ought to cultivate ritual and music; then with dukes and ministers jointly deliberated and fixed south and north suburb caps and carriages and robes system, and Guangwu temple ascent songs eight-row dance numbers—speech is in Ritual Music and Carriages and Robes treatises.
translations.idiomatic: [41] Thirty years into the restoration the realm was calm; Cang urged ritual reform and with the high ministers set suburban dress and music for the Guangwu temple—the detail is in the treatises on ritual and carriages.

sentences.zh: [42]帝每巡狩，蒼常留鎮，侍衞皇太后。

translations.literal: [42] Whenever the emperor toured hunts, Cang always remained to garrison; attended and guarded empress dowager.
translations.idiomatic: [42] On each imperial tour Cang stayed behind to guard the capital and attend the empress dowager.

sentences.zh: 四年春，車駕近出，觀覽城第，[43]尋聞當遂校獵河內，蒼即上書諫曰：「臣聞時令，盛春農事，不聚觿興功。

translations.literal: Fourth year spring, imperial carriage near went out, viewing city mansions, [43] soon heard would follow hunting drill Henei; Cang immediately submitted memorial remonstrating, saying: "Your servant hears seasonal ordinance—flourishing spring farming affairs—do not gather for drilling or raise works.
translations.idiomatic: In spring of the fourth year the emperor toured the capital mansions note forty-three in the source and planned a hunt in Henei; Cang memorialized: "Spring is for the plow—do not mobilize drill or works."

sentences.zh: [44]傳曰：『田獵不宿，食飲不享，出入不節，則木不曲直。

translations.literal: [44] Tradition says: 'Field hunt not overnight; food drink not feast; going out and entering not regulated—then wood does not bend straight.
translations.idiomatic: [44] The tradition says: When hunt and feast break spring discipline, the phase of wood suffers."

sentences.zh: 』此失春令者也。

translations.literal: ' This is losing spring ordinance.
translations.idiomatic: That is how spring ordinance is lost."

sentences.zh: [45]臣知車駕今出，事從約省，所過吏人諷誦甘棠之德。

translations.literal: [45] Your servant knows imperial carriage now goes out—affairs follow reduction and economy—those passed clerks and people chant sweet pear-tree virtue.
translations.idiomatic: [45] I know Your Majesty travels lightly and the people praise your kindness like the ode of the pear tree."

sentences.zh: 雖然，動不以禮，非所以示四方也。

translations.literal: Although so—movement not by ritual is not the means to display to four directions.
translations.idiomatic: Yet motion without ritual teaches the empire nothing."

sentences.zh: 惟陛下因行田野，循視稼穡，消搖仿佯，弭節而旋。

translations.literal: Only wish Your Majesty ride along fields; follow and view crops; wander roam; rein pace and return.
translations.idiomatic: Ride the countryside, inspect the crops, then turn home at leisure."

sentences.zh: [46]至秋冬，乃振威靈，整法駕，備周衞，設羽旄。

translations.literal: [46] When reaching autumn winter—then rouse awe-inspiring spirit; arrange statutory carriage; prepare thorough guards; set feather banners.
translations.idiomatic: [46] Save full hunts for autumn and winter with proper escort and banners."

sentences.zh: [47]詩云：『抑抑威儀，惟德之隅。

translations.literal: [47] Poetry says: 'Stately awful deportment—only virtue's corner.
translations.idiomatic: [47] The Poetry says: Majestic bearing shows where virtue dwells."

sentences.zh: 』[48]臣不勝憤懣，伏自手書，乞詣行在所，極陳至誠。

translations.literal: ' [48] Your servant cannot overcome indignant depression; humbly personally hand-wrote; begging to go to traveling lodge; exhaustively display utmost sincerity.
translations.idiomatic: [48] I write myself and beg audience wherever the court resides to speak my whole heart."

sentences.zh: 」帝覽奏，即還宮。

translations.literal: " The emperor read memorial; immediately returned palace.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor read the memorial and returned to the palace.

sentences.zh: 蒼在朝數載，多所隆益，而自以至親輔政，聲望日重，意不自安，上疏歸職曰：

translations.literal: Cang at court several years—much enriched and benefited; yet himself because utmost kin assisting government—reputation daily heavy—intent not self secure—submitted memorial returning duties, saying:
translations.idiomatic: Cang served at court for years with great benefit, but as the emperor's closest kin his stature grew unsettling; he asked to resign:

sentences.zh: 臣蒼疲駑，特為陛下慈恩覆護，在家備教導之仁，升朝蒙爵命之首，制書襃美，班之四海，舉負薪之才，升君子之器。

translations.literal: Your servant Cang tired and stupid—specially by Your Majesty's kind grace covered and protected; at home prepared instructing benevolence; ascending court received noble mandate's head; system writings praised beauty—distributed to four seas—raised firewood-bearing talent—elevated to gentleman's vessel.
translations.idiomatic: Your servant Cang is undistinguished yet you shelter me—at home you teach me; at court you rank me first among peers and broadcast praise across the empire—you promote a woodcutter to the bronze vessel."

sentences.zh: [49]凡匹夫一介，尚不忘簞食之惠，[50]況臣居宰相之位，同氣之親哉！

translations.literal: [49] All common man single duty—still does not forget bamboo-food kindness; [50] how much more your servant occupying minister's position—same-breath kin!
translations.idiomatic: [49] Common men repay a meal note forty-nine in the source note fifty in the source—I hold ministerial rank and share your breath."

sentences.zh: 宜當暴骸膏野，為百僚先，而愚頑之質，加以固病，誠羞負乘，辱污輔將之位，將被詩人『三百赤紱』之刺。

translations.literal: Ought to expose bones fat field—be hundred officials' head—yet foolish stubborn substance—added chronic illness—truly ashamed burden riding—disgrace and soil assistant general's position—will receive poets' 'three hundred crimson seals' satire.
translations.idiomatic: I should die in the field leading officials; instead I am sick and weak—a donkey carrying relics on the minister's seat, ripe for the poets' mockery of venal lords."

sentences.zh: [51]今方域晏然，要荒無儆，[52]將遵上德無為之時也，文官猶可並省，武職尤不宜建。

translations.literal: [51] Now regions tranquil; remote wastes no alert—[52] about to follow superior virtue non-action's time—civil officials still can merge and reduce; military posts especially not fitting to establish.
translations.idiomatic: [51] The realm is quiet and the frontiers need no alarm note fifty-two in the source—this is the age of ruling through nonaction; civil posts may be cut and military offices should not multiply."

sentences.zh: 昔象封有鼻，不任以政，[53]誠由愛深，不忍揚其過惡。

sentences.zh: 前事之不忘，來事之師也。

translations.literal: Former affairs not forgotten—coming affairs' teacher.
translations.idiomatic: The past is the teacher of the future."

sentences.zh: 惟陛下審覽虞帝優養母弟，遵承舊典，終卒厚恩。

translations.literal: Only wish Your Majesty carefully examine Shun's nurturing mother's younger brother; follow and inherit old statutes; finish thick grace.

sentences.zh: 乞上驃騎將軍印綬，退就蕃國，願蒙哀憐。

translations.idiomatic: I beg to return the seals of general of agile cavalry and retire to my fief—grant me pity."

sentences.zh: 帝優詔不聽。

translations.idiomatic: The emperor issued a kind refusal.

sentences.zh: 其後數陳乞，辭甚懇切。

translations.literal: Afterward several times stated begging—words very earnest and urgent.
translations.idiomatic: He pleaded again and again with mounting urgency.

translations.idiomatic: In the fifth year he was allowed home but kept his general's commission.

sentences.zh: 以驃騎長史為東平太傅，掾為中大夫，令史為王家郎。

translations.idiomatic: His chief clerk became grand tutor of East Peace, his clerks grand counselors and household gentlemen.

sentences.zh: [54]加賜錢五千萬，布十萬匹。

translations.idiomatic: [54] He received fifty million cash and one hundred thousand bolts of cloth.

sentences.zh: 六年冬，帝幸魯，徵蒼從還京師。

translations.literal: Sixth year winter, emperor favored Lu; summoned Cang to follow return capital.
translations.idiomatic: That winter the emperor visited Lu and brought Cang back to the capital.

sentences.zh: 明年，皇太后崩。

translations.literal: Next year, empress dowager collapsed.
translations.idiomatic: The following year the empress dowager died.

sentences.zh: 既葬，蒼乃歸國，特賜宮人奴婢五百人，布二十五萬匹，及珍寶服御器物。

translations.literal: After burial, Cang then returned state; specially granted palace women slave servants five hundred persons; cloth two hundred fifty thousand bolts; and precious treasures garments and traveling vessels.
translations.idiomatic: After the funeral Cang went home with five hundred maids, two hundred fifty thousand bolts of cloth, and treasures.

sentences.zh: 十一年，蒼與諸王朝京師。

translations.literal: In the eleventh year, Cang with various kings courted capital.
translations.idiomatic: In year 11 Cang joined the other kings at court.

sentences.zh: 月餘，還國。

translations.literal: More than a month; returned state.
translations.idiomatic: After a month he returned to his fief.

sentences.zh: 帝臨送歸宮，淒然懷思，乃遣使手詔國中傅曰：「辭別之後，獨坐不樂，因就車歸，伏軾而吟，瞻望永懷，實勞我心，誦及采菽，以增歎息。

translations.literal: Emperor attended sending return palace—sadly cherished thought—then dispatched envoy handwritten edict to state tutor, saying: "After parting words—sitting alone not joyful—thereupon took carriage return; leaned on crossbar and chanted; gazed afar eternal longing—truly wearies my heart—recite reaching Gathering Beans—to increase sighing.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor escorted him and then wrote his tutor: "Since you left I sit alone unhappy; riding home I leaned on the carriage rail and thought of you—I turn to the ode Gathering Beans and sigh."

sentences.zh: [55]日者問東平王處家何等最樂，王言為善最樂，其言甚大，副是要腹矣。

translations.literal: [55] The other day asked East Peace king dwelling home what most joyful—king said doing good most joyful—his words very great—match this waist belly.
translations.idiomatic: [55] I asked what pleased you most at home—you said doing good; those words hit the mark."

sentences.zh: 今送列侯印十九枚，諸王子年五歲已上能趨拜者，皆令帶之。」

translations.literal: " Now sending full marquis seals nineteen pieces—various king's sons age five upward able to hurry and bow—all ordered wear them."
translations.idiomatic: Here are nineteen full marquis seals—every prince aged five or older who can bow shall wear one."

sentences.zh: 十五年春，行幸東平，賜蒼錢千五百萬，布四萬匹。

translations.idiomatic: In spring of year 15 the emperor visited East Peace and gave Cang fifteen million cash and forty thousand bolts of cloth.

sentences.zh: 帝以所作光武本紀示蒼，蒼因上光武受命中興頌。

translations.idiomatic: The emperor showed Cang his Guangwu annals; Cang presented an eulogy on Guangwu's restoration.

sentences.zh: 帝甚善之，以其文典雅，特令校書郎賈逵為之訓詁。

translations.literal: Emperor greatly approved it—because its text refined elegant—specially ordered collating clerk Jia Kui for it do glosses.
translations.idiomatic: Delighted by its classical tone, the emperor had Jia Kui annotate it.

sentences.zh: 肅宗即位，尊重恩禮踰於前世，諸王莫與為比。

translations.literal: Emperor Zhang ascended throne—respect and honor grace ritual exceeded former generations—various kings none could compare with him.
translations.idiomatic: When Emperor Zhang succeeded he honored Cang beyond any earlier king.

sentences.zh: 建初元年，地震，蒼上便宜，其事留中。

translations.idiomatic: In Jianchu 1 an earthquake struck; Cang sent practical proposals kept inside the palace.

translations.literal: On Bingyin submitted convenience three matters—I personally read and examined—repeated several rounds—heart opened eyes bright—vastly lifted darkness.

sentences.zh: 後帝欲為原陵、顯節陵起縣邑，蒼聞之，遽上疏諫曰：「伏聞當為二陵起立郭邑，臣前頗謂道路之言，疑不審實，近令從官古霸問湼陽主疾，[59]使還，乃知詔書已下。

translations.literal: I privately see Emperor Guangwu personally practiced frugal conduct; deeply grasped the distinction from life to death; diligent and earnest; spoke on burial regulations; therefore when building the tomb site fully cited classical models; edict said 'Do not build a mountain mound—ponds only wide enough for flowing water.'

translations.literal: [61] Your servant foolishly thinks park-town's rise—began from violent Qin.

translations.idiomatic: By every popular rule of lucky and unlucky timing you should not alter tombs without cause."

translations.idiomatic: It fits neither ancient precedent nor present good sense, and promises no blessing."

translations.literal: Third year, emperor feasted guards at Southern Palace; thereby followed empress dowager touring around Yeeting ponds and towers; then inspected Lady Yin empress dowager's former utensils and garments—sorrowfully moved expression—then ordered retain five-seasons garments each one set; [64] and constantly worn imperial garments totaling fifty chests—remainder all distributed various kings and princesses and descendants at capital—each had difference.
translations.idiomatic: In year 3 the emperor feasted the guards at the Southern Palace and walked the empress dowager through the inner palace pools and galleries. Seeing Lady Yin's old robes he wept and kept one outfit from each season note sixty-four in the source plus fifty chests of everyday wear, dividing the rest among princes and princesses still at court."

translations.idiomatic: He wrote specially to Cang and King Jing of Langye: "Your messenger brought word of you—I cannot praise you enough!"

translations.idiomatic: The years race by; the tombs grow distant—my heart aches—what can I say!"

translations.idiomatic: Surely a prince of your filial devotion feels the same!"

translations.idiomatic: [69] Guard your health and take care of yourself."

translations.idiomatic: That winter Cang asked leave to visit court.

translations.literal: " As for Pei, Jinan, East Peace, Zhongshan four kings—in praising all do not name."
translations.idiomatic: Do not speak the personal names of the kings of Pei, Jinan, East Peace, and Zhongshan when addressing them."

sentences.zh: [74]蒼既至，升殿乃拜，天子親荅之。

translations.literal: [74] When Cang arrived—ascended hall then bowed—Son of Heaven personally replied.
translations.idiomatic: [74] When Cang arrived he bowed on the hall steps and the emperor answered in person.

sentences.zh: 其後諸王入宮，輒以輦迎，至省合乃下。

translations.literal: Afterward various kings entered palace—always with palanquin welcome—reaching secretariat compound then descended.
translations.idiomatic: Later kings were carried in palanquins to the inner offices before they walked.

sentences.zh: 蒼以受恩過禮，情不自寧，上疏辭曰：「臣聞貴有常尊，賤有等威，[75]卑高列序，上下以理。

translations.literal: Cang because receiving grace exceeded ritual—emotion not self at peace—submitted memorial declining, saying: "Your servant hears noble has constant honor; base has ranked awe; [75] low high array order—upper lower by pattern.
translations.idiomatic: Overwhelmed by favor, Cang wrote: "Rank has its proper dignity and humility its measure note seventy-five in the source—high and low stand in order."

sentences.zh: 陛下至德廣施，慈愛骨肉，既賜奉朝請，咫尺天儀，而親屈至尊，降禮下臣，每賜燕見，輒興席改容，中宮親拜，事過典故。

translations.literal: Your Majesty utmost virtue broadly extends—loving kindness flesh and bone—already granted court audience privilege—foot zhang heaven's demeanor—yet personally bends utmost honor—lowers ritual to inferior ministers—each grants banquet audience—always rises mat changes expression—central palace personally bows—affair surpasses old precedents.
translations.idiomatic: Your majesty loves your kin yet grants me audience at arm's length, rises when we dine, lets the empress bow—every kindness exceeds precedent."

sentences.zh: 臣惶怖戰慄，誠不自安，每會見，踧踖無所措置。

translations.literal: Your servant fearful frightened trembling—truly not self secure—each meeting audience—cramped cramped nowhere to place.
translations.idiomatic: I tremble at each audience—I cannot steady myself."

sentences.zh: [76]此非所以章示腢下，安臣子也。」

translations.literal: [76] This is not the means to manifest display foot-subordinates—settle ministers and sons."
translations.idiomatic: [76] This is no way to show the realm how to quiet ministers and sons."

sentences.zh: 帝省奏歎息，愈襃貴焉。

translations.literal: Emperor examined memorial sighed—more praised and honored him.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor read the memorial and sighed—then prized him all the more.

sentences.zh: 舊典，諸王女皆封鄉主，乃獨封蒼五女為縣公主。

translations.literal: Old statute—various kings' daughters all enfeoffed village ladies—then alone enfeoffed Cang's five daughters as county princesses.
translations.idiomatic: By statute princes' daughters were village ladies—only Cang's five daughters were made county princesses.

sentences.zh: 三月，大鴻臚奏遣諸王歸國，帝特留蒼，賜以秘書、列僊圖、道術秘方。

translations.literal: Third month, grand herald memorialized dispatch various kings return states—emperor specially retained Cang—granted him secret books, arrayed immortal charts, Dao arts secret recipes.
translations.idiomatic: In the third month the grand herald sent the kings home but kept Cang, gifting him rare books, Daoist charts, and secret formulas.

sentences.zh: 至八月飲酎畢，[77]有司復奏遣蒼，乃許之。

translations.literal: When eighth month drank infusion finished; [77] authorities again memorialized dispatch Cang—then permitted it.
translations.idiomatic: After the autumn libation note seventy-seven in the source the ministry again asked Cang to leave—this time the emperor agreed.

sentences.zh: 手詔賜蒼曰：「骨肉天性，誠不以遠近為親疏，然數見顏色，情重昔時。

translations.literal: Hand edict granted Cang, saying: "Flesh and bone heaven nature—truly not by far near as intimate distant—yet repeatedly seeing face color—emotion heavy past times.
translations.idiomatic: A personal letter said: "Kinship knows no distance, yet seeing you often bound us closer than before."

sentences.zh: 念王久勞，思得還休，欲署大鴻臚奏，不忍下筆，顧授小黃門，中心戀戀，惻然不能言。

translations.literal: Thinking king long labored—thinking obtain return rest—wish to sign grand herald memorial—cannot bear put down brush—turning hand to minor yellow gate—center heart lingering—sorrowful cannot speak.
translations.idiomatic: Knowing you are weary I meant to sign your dismissal myself but could not bear to write—I handed the brush to a eunuch; my heart clings and I cannot speak."

sentences.zh: 」[78]於是車駕祖送，流涕而訣。

translations.literal: " [78] Thereupon imperial carriage libation sending—weeping tears and farewell.
translations.idiomatic: [78] The emperor offered the farewell sacrifice and wept at parting.

sentences.zh: 復賜乘輿服御，珍寶輿馬，錢布以億萬計。

translations.literal: Again granted riding carriage garments and travel—precious treasures carriage horses—cash cloth counted by hundreds of millions.
translations.idiomatic: More gifts followed—carriages, jewels, horses, cash beyond counting.

sentences.zh: 蒼還國，疾病，帝馳遣名醫，小黃門侍疾，使者冠蓋不絕於道。

translations.literal: Cang returned state—ill; emperor galloped dispatch famous physicians—minor yellow gates attended illness—envoys capped coaches unceasing on road.
translations.idiomatic: Back in his kingdom Cang fell ill; the emperor rushed physicians and eunuch nurses—messengers crowded the road.

sentences.zh: 又置驛馬千里，傳問起居。

translations.literal: Also set relay horses thousand li—transmit inquire rising resting.
translations.idiomatic: Relay riders carried inquiries a thousand li without pause.

sentences.zh: 明年正月薨，詔告中傅，封上蒼自建武以來章奏及所作書、記、賦、頌、七言、別字、歌詩，並集覽焉。

translations.literal: Next year first month died—edict informed tutor—sealed submit Cang from Jianwu onward memorials and composed documents, records, rhapsodies, eulogies, seven-character lines, variant characters, songs and poems—together collected and perused.
translations.idiomatic: He died the next first month; the court ordered his tutor to seal every memorial and literary work since Jianwu for the imperial library.

sentences.zh: 遣大鴻臚持節，五官中郎將副監喪，及將作使者凡六人，令四姓小侯諸國王主悉會詣東平奔喪，賜錢前後一億，布九萬匹。

translations.literal: Dispatched grand herald holding credentials; general of the household for all purposes as deputy supervised mourning; and court architect envoys altogether six persons—ordered four-surname minor marquises various kingdoms kings princesses all assemble go East Peace rush mourning—granted cash before and after one hundred million; cloth ninety thousand bolts.
translations.idiomatic: The grand herald directed the funeral with six officers; nobles of every rank were summoned to East Peace; the grants totaled one hundred million cash and ninety thousand bolts of cloth.

sentences.zh: 及葬，策曰：「惟建初八年三月己卯，皇帝曰：咨王丕顯，勤勞王室，親受策命，昭於前世。

translations.literal: When burial—document said: "Only Jianchu eighth year third month jimao—emperor says: Oh king greatly manifest—toiled royal house—personally received document mandate—bright in former generation.
translations.idiomatic: The burial edict for Jianchu 8 read: "King, you shone in service and bore your mandate in life."

sentences.zh: 出作蕃輔，克慎明德，率禮不越，[79]傅聞在下。

translations.literal: Going out as feudal assistant—able careful bright virtue—lead ritual not transgress; [79] transmission-hearing lies below.
translations.idiomatic: As a feudal bulwark you kept ritual note seventy-nine in the source—your fame reached the common folk."

sentences.zh: [80]昊天不吊，不報上仁，俾屏余一人，夙夜殺殺，靡有所終。

translations.literal: [80] Vast Heaven not pity—not repay superior humanity—make screen me alone—morning night sorrow sorrow—have nowhere ending.
translations.idiomatic: [80] Heaven shows no pity—it denies me your counsel and leaves me screening alone in endless grief."

sentences.zh: [81]今詔有司加賜鸞輅乘馬，龍旗九旒，虎賁百人，奉送王行。

translations.literal: [81] Now order responsible offices additionally grant phoenix carriage riding horses—dragon banner nine streamers—tiger guards hundred persons—offer sending king journey.
translations.idiomatic: [81] The ministry adds a phoenix carriage, dragon banners, nine pennants, and a hundred guards for the cortège."

sentences.zh: 匪我憲王，其孰離之！

translations.literal: Not our King Xian—who else could part him!
translations.idiomatic: Who but our King Xian could bear this parting!"

sentences.zh: [82]魂而有靈，保茲寵榮。

translations.literal: [82] If soul has spirit—preserve this favor glory.
translations.idiomatic: [82] May your spirit keep this glory."

sentences.zh: 嗚呼哀哉！」

translations.literal: Alas sorrow!"
translations.idiomatic: Alas!"

sentences.zh: 立四十五年，子懷王忠嗣。

translations.literal: Established forty-five years; son King Huai Zhong succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: He ruled forty-five years; King Huai Zhong succeeded.

sentences.zh: 明年，帝乃分東平國封忠弟尚為任城王，餘五人為列侯。

translations.literal: Next year, emperor then divided East Peace state—enfeoffed Zhong's younger brother Shang as king of Rencheng—remaining five persons became full marquises.
translations.idiomatic: The next year the emperor split East Peace to make Shang king of Rencheng and five brothers full marquises.

sentences.zh: 敞立四十八年薨，子頃王端嗣。

translations.literal: Chang established forty-eight years and died; son King Qing Duan succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: Chang ruled forty-eight years; King Qing Duan succeeded.

sentences.zh: 立四十七年薨，子凱嗣；

translations.literal: Established forty-seven years and died; son Kai succeeded;
translations.idiomatic: He ruled forty-seven years; Kai succeeded;

sentences.zh: 立四十一年，魏受禪，以為崇德侯。

translations.literal: Established forty-one years; Wei received abdication; was made Marquis of Honoring Virtue.
translations.idiomatic: After forty-one years the Wei accepted the abdication and named him marquis of Chongde.

sentences.zh: 論曰：孔子稱「貧而無諂，富而無驕，未若貧而樂，富而好禮者也」。

translations.literal: Judgment says: Confucius called 'Poor yet not flattering—rich yet not proud—not like poor yet joyful—rich yet loving ritual.'
translations.idiomatic: Appreciation: Confucius said it is better to be poor and content or rich and ritual-minded than merely free of flattery or pride."

sentences.zh: 若東平憲王，可謂好禮者也。

translations.literal: As for King Xian of East Peace—may be called one who loved ritual.
translations.idiomatic: King Xian of East Peace was such a man.

sentences.zh: 若其辭至戚，去母后，豈欲苟立名行而忘親遺義哉！

translations.literal: As for his declining utmost kin—leaving empress dowager—could he wish rashly to establish name conduct yet forget kin abandon righteousness!
translations.idiomatic: When he stepped back from kin and dowager he sought no empty fame—he did not betray duty."

sentences.zh: 蓋位疑則隙生，累近則喪大，[84]斯蓋明哲之所為歎息。

translations.literal: When position is doubtful then crack arises; burden near then loss great—[84] this is what bright wise ones sigh over.
translations.idiomatic: Suspicion breeds rifts; closeness courts ruin note eighty-four in the source—the wise have always lamented it."

sentences.zh: 嗚呼！

translations.literal: Alas!
translations.idiomatic: Alas!

sentences.zh: 遠隙以全忠，釋累以成孝，夫豈憲王之志哉！

translations.literal: Far crack to preserve loyalty—release burden to complete filiality—how could it be King Xian's intent!
translations.idiomatic: Distance heals suspicion and yielding saves filial debt—that was King Xian's meaning!"

sentences.zh: [85]東海恭王遜而知廢，[86]「為吳太伯，不亦可乎」！

translations.literal: [85] East Sea King Gong yielded and knew abandonment; [86] 'Act Wu Taibo—is it not also acceptable!'
translations.idiomatic: [85] King Gong of the East Sea stepped aside knowing the cost note eighty-six in the source—like Taibo of Wu."

translations.literal: Editorial mark eighty-seven in the received text.
translations.idiomatic: Editorial note eighty-seven in the source.

sentences.zh: 任城孝王尚，元和元年封，食任城、亢父、樊三縣。

translations.literal: King Xiao of Rencheng Shang—in first year of Yuanhe enfeoffed—ate Rencheng, Kangfu, Fan three counties.
translations.idiomatic: King Xiao of Rencheng, Liu Shang, was enfeoffed in Yuanhe 1 with the three counties Rencheng, Kangfu, and Fan.

translations.literal: Editorial mark eighty-eight in the received text.
translations.idiomatic: Editorial note eighty-eight in the source.

sentences.zh: 安立十九年薨，子節王崇嗣。

translations.literal: An established nineteen years and died; son King Jie Chong succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: An ruled nineteen years; King Jie Chong succeeded.

sentences.zh: 順帝時，羌虜數反，崇輒上錢帛佐邊費。

translations.literal: Emperor Shun's time—Qiang captives several times rebelled; Chong then submitted cash and silk assisting border expense.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Shun when the Qiang rose repeatedly, Chong sent cash and silk for the frontier.

sentences.zh: 及帝崩，復上錢三百萬助山陵用度，朝廷嘉而不受。

translations.literal: When emperor collapsed—again submitted cash three million assisting mound hills expense—court praised but did not accept.
translations.idiomatic: When the emperor died he offered three million for the tomb; the court praised him but declined.

sentences.zh: 立三十一年薨，無子，國絕。

translations.literal: Established thirty-one years and died; no son; state cut off.
translations.idiomatic: He ruled thirty-one years without an heir; the kingdom lapsed.

sentences.zh: 延熹四年，桓帝立河閒孝王子*

translations.literal: In fourth year of Yanxi, Emperor Huan established Hejian Prince Xiao's son —
translations.idiomatic: In Yanxi 4 Emperor Huan raised the son of Prince Xiao of Hejian—

sentences.zh: (恭為)

translations.literal: (Gong wei)
translations.idiomatic: Variant wording inserts Gong here in some editions.

sentences.zh: *參戶亭侯博為任城王，以奉其祀。

translations.literal: —Village Marquis of Canhu Bo as king of Rencheng—to continue its sacrifices.
translations.idiomatic: —Bo, village marquis of Canhu, as king of Rencheng to maintain the line.

translations.literal: Editorial mark eighty-nine in the received text.
translations.idiomatic: Editorial note eighty-nine in the source.

sentences.zh: 博有孝行，喪母服制如禮，增封三千戶。

translations.literal: Bo had filial conduct—mourning mother garment system like ritual—increased enfeoffment three thousand households.
translations.idiomatic: Bo mourned his mother by the book and gained three thousand households.

sentences.zh: 立十三年薨，無子，國絕。

translations.literal: Established thirteen years and died; no son; state cut off.
translations.idiomatic: He ruled thirteen years without an heir; the kingdom lapsed.

sentences.zh: 熹平四年，靈帝復立河閒貞王*

translations.literal: In fourth year of Xiping, Emperor Ling again established Hejian Prince Zhen —
translations.idiomatic: In Xiping 4 Emperor Ling restored the line of Prince Zhen of Hejian—

translations.literal: (Xun)
translations.idiomatic: Some editions read the prince's name as Xun.

translations.literal: ** [Jian]** [zi]* Marquis of Xinchang —
translations.idiomatic: Manuscript variants tag Jian or zi before marquis of Xinchang.

translations.literal: (zi)
translations.idiomatic: Alternate gloss son.

sentences.zh: *佗為任城王，奉孝王后。

translations.literal: —Tuo as king of Rencheng—serve King Xiao's posterity.
translations.idiomatic: —Tuo as king of Rencheng to continue King Xiao's sacrifices.

sentences.zh: 立四十六年，魏受禪，以為崇德侯。

translations.literal: Established forty-six years; Wei received abdication; was made Marquis of Honoring Virtue.
translations.idiomatic: After forty-six years the Wei named him marquis of Chongde.

sentences.zh: 阜陵質王延

translations.literal: King Zhi of Fuling Yan.
translations.idiomatic: King Zhi of Fuling, Liu Yan

sentences.zh: 阜陵質王延，建武十五年封淮陽公，十七年進爵為王，二十八年就國。

translations.literal: King Zhi of Fuling Yan—in fifteenth year of Jianwu enfeoffed Huaiyang duke; seventeenth year advanced rank to king; twenty-eighth year went to state.
translations.idiomatic: King Zhi of Fuling was duke of Huaiyang in Jianwu 15, king in Jianwu 17, and took his fief in Jianwu 28.

sentences.zh: 三十年，以汝南之長平、西華、新陽、扶樂四縣益淮陽國。

translations.literal: Thirtieth year—taking Runan's Changping, Xihua, Xinyang, Fule four counties to augment Huaiyang state.
translations.idiomatic: In Jianwu 30 four Runan counties were added to Huaiyang.

translations.literal: Editorial mark ninety in the received text.
translations.idiomatic: Editorial note ninety in the source.

sentences.zh: 延性驕奢而遇下嚴烈。

translations.literal: Yan's nature was arrogant and luxurious yet treated inferiors harsh and severe.
translations.idiomatic: Yan was arrogant and harsh with subordinates.

sentences.zh: 永平中，有上書告延與姬兄謝弇及姊館陶主婿駙馬都尉韓光招奸猾，作圖讖，祠祭祝詛。

translations.literal: In Yongping, there was memorial accusing Yan with concubine elder brother Xie Yan and elder sister Guantao princess son-in-law chief equerry Han Guang summoning crafty men—making chart-weft prophecies—shrine sacrifice prayer curse.
translations.idiomatic: Under Yongping informers charged Yan with his concubine's brother Xie Yan and his sister's husband Han Guang for recruiting plotters, forging prognostications, and dark prayers.

sentences.zh: 事下案驗，光、弇被殺，辭所連及，死徙者甚觿。

translations.literal: Affair descended case verification—Guang, Yan were killed—statements what linked reached—dead and exiled very many.
translations.idiomatic: Investigators executed Han and Xie; confessions implicated many who died or were banished.

sentences.zh: 有司奏請誅延。

translations.literal: Authorities memorialized requesting execute Yan.
translations.idiomatic: The ministry asked for Yan's death.

sentences.zh: 顯宗以延罪薄於楚王英，故特加恩，徙為阜陵王，食二縣。

translations.literal: Emperor Ming because Yan's crimes thinner than Chu King Ying's—therefore specially added grace—transferred to be Fuling king—ate two counties.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Ming judged Yan less guilty than King Ying of Chu and spared him as king of Fuling with two counties.

sentences.zh: 延既徙封，數懷怨望。

translations.literal: After Yan was transferred enfeoffment—several times harbored resentment and gaze.
translations.idiomatic: After his transfer Yan nursed grievances.

sentences.zh: 建初中，復有告延與子男魴造逆謀者，有司奏請檻車徵詣廷尉詔獄。

translations.literal: In Jianchu period—again had accusation Yan with son male Fang making rebellious plot—authorities memorialized requesting caged cart summon to commandant of justice imperial prison.
translations.idiomatic: During Jianchu informers charged Yan and his son Fang with conspiracy; the ministry asked to haul them to the imperial prison in a cage cart.

sentences.zh: 肅宗下詔曰：「王前犯大逆，罪惡尤深，有同周之管、蔡，漢之淮南。

translations.literal: Emperor Zhang issued edict saying: "King formerly committed great rebellion—crime evil especially deep—same as Zhou's Guan and Cai—Han Huainan.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Zhang said: "The king's earlier treason matched the Guan and Cai of Zhou or Huainan under Han."

sentences.zh: [91]經有正義，律有明刑。

translations.literal: [91] Classics have correct meaning—statutes have clear punishments.
translations.idiomatic: [91] The canon demands justice; the code prescribes punishment."

sentences.zh: [92]先帝不忍親親之恩，枉屈大法，為王受愆，[93]腢下莫不惑焉。

translations.literal: [92] Former emperor could not bear loving-kin grace—bent great law—undertook guilt for king—[93] foot-subordinates none not perplexed.
translations.idiomatic: [92] The late emperor spared you for kinship and bent the law note ninety-three in the source—the whole court wondered at it."

sentences.zh: 今王曾莫悔悟，悖心不移，逆謀內潰，自子魴發，誠非本朝之所樂聞。

translations.literal: Now king never repented awakening—perverse heart not moved—rebellious plot inward collapsed—from son Fang issued—truly not this court's joy to hear.
translations.idiomatic: Now you show no remorse; treason festers until your son Fang exposes it—no joy for this court."

sentences.zh: 朕惻然傷心，不忍致王於理，今貶爵為阜陵侯，食一縣。

translations.literal: I sorrowfully wound heart—cannot bear deliver king to judgment—now demote rank to Fuling marquis—eat one county.
translations.idiomatic: My heart breaks—I cannot try you as a traitor: you are demoted to marquis of Fuling with one county.

sentences.zh: 獲斯辜者，侯自取焉。

translations.literal: Obtain this guilt—marquis himself took it.
translations.idiomatic: You brought this blame on yourself.

sentences.zh: 於戲誡哉！

translations.literal: Oh alas warning!
translations.idiomatic: Take heed!"

sentences.zh: 」赦魴等罪勿驗，使謁者一人監護延國，不得與吏人通。

translations.literal: " Pardon Fang and others' crimes—do not verify—ordered one herald supervise guard Yan state—not permitted associate with clerks and people.
translations.idiomatic: " Fang and the rest are pardoned without trial; one herald will oversee your kingdom—you may not meet officials."

sentences.zh: 章和元年，行幸九江，賜延書與車駕會壽春。

translations.literal: First year of Zhanghe—traveled favoring Jiujiang—granted Yan letter ordering join imperial carriage at Shouchun.
translations.idiomatic: In Zhanghe 1 the emperor toured Jiujiang and summoned Yan to meet him at Shouchun.

sentences.zh: 帝見延及妻子，愍然傷之，乃下詔曰：「昔周之爵封千有八百，而姬姓居半者，所以楨榦王室也。

translations.literal: Emperor saw Yan and wife children—pained wounded him—then issued edict saying: "Formerly Zhou's noble enfeoffments thousand eight hundred—and Ji surname occupied half—thereby was ridgepole royal house.
translations.idiomatic: Seeing Yan with his family the emperor grieved and proclaimed: "Zhou once had eighteen hundred fiefs—half were Ji—to buttress the throne."

sentences.zh: 朕南巡，望淮、海，意在阜陵，遂與侯相見。

translations.literal: I south toured—gazed Huai, sea—intent at Fuling—thereupon with marquis met.
translations.idiomatic: I marched south toward Huai and the sea hoping to see Fuling—and met you, marquis."

sentences.zh: 侯志意衰落，形體非故，瞻省懷感，以喜以悲。

translations.literal: Marquis intent will declining wasted—form body not former—look inspect bosom moved—with joy with grief.
translations.idiomatic: Your spirit flags and your body is changed—seeing you moves me to joy and sorrow."

sentences.zh: 今復侯為阜陵王，增封四縣，並前為五縣。

translations.literal: Now restore marquis as Fuling king—increase enfeoffment four counties—together before five counties.
translations.idiomatic: I restore you as king of Fuling with four new counties—five in all."

sentences.zh: 」以阜陵下濕，徙都壽春，加賜錢千萬，布萬匹，安車一乘，夫人諸子賞賜各有差。

translations.literal: " Because Fuling low damp—moved capital Shouchun—additionally granted cash ten million; cloth ten thousand bolts; secure carriage one ride—lady and various sons gifts grants each had difference.
translations.idiomatic: " Because Fuling was damp the seat moves to Shouchun—with ten million cash, ten thousand bolts, one carriage, and graded gifts for wife and sons."

sentences.zh: 明年入朝。

translations.literal: Next year entered court.
translations.idiomatic: The next year he attended court.

sentences.zh: 立五十一年薨，子殤王沖嗣。

translations.literal: Established fifty-one years died; son King Shang Chong succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: He ruled fifty-one years; King Shang Chong succeeded.

sentences.zh: 永元二年，下詔盡削除前班下延事。

translations.literal: Second year of Yongyuan—issued edict entirely delete erase former issued Yan affair.
translations.idiomatic: In Yongyuan 2 an edict annulled every earlier decree against Yan.

sentences.zh: 沖立二年薨，無嗣。

translations.literal: Chong established two years died; no heir.
translations.idiomatic: Chong ruled two years without an heir.

sentences.zh: 和帝復封沖兄魴，是為頃王。

translations.literal: Emperor He again enfeoffed Chong's elder brother Fang—this is King Qing.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor He restored Fang, Chong's elder brother, as King Qing.

sentences.zh: 永元八年，封魴弟十二人為鄉、亭侯。

translations.literal: Eighth year of Yongyuan—Fang's twelve younger brothers enfeoffed village and pavilion marquises.
translations.idiomatic: In Yongyuan 8 twelve of Fang's brothers became village or pavilion marquises.

sentences.zh: 魴立三十年薨，子懷王恢嗣。

translations.literal: Fang established thirty years died; son King Huai Hui succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: Fang ruled thirty years; King Huai Hui succeeded.

sentences.zh: 延光三年，封恢兄弟五人為鄉、亭侯。

translations.literal: Third year of Yanguang—Hui's five brothers enfeoffed village and pavilion marquises.
translations.idiomatic: In Yanguang 3 five of Hui's brothers became village or pavilion marquises.

sentences.zh: 恢立十年薨，子節王代嗣。

translations.literal: Hui established ten years died; son King Jie Dai succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: Hui ruled ten years; King Jie Dai succeeded.

sentences.zh: 陽嘉二年，封代兄便親為勃遒亭侯。

translations.literal: Second year of Yangjia—Dai's elder brother Bianqin enfeoffed pavilion marquis of Boqiu.
translations.idiomatic: In Yangjia 2 Dai's elder brother Bianqin became pavilion marquis of Boqiu.

sentences.zh: 代立十四年薨，無子，國絕。

translations.literal: Dai established fourteen years died; no son; state cut off.
translations.idiomatic: Dai ruled fourteen years without an heir; the kingdom lapsed.

sentences.zh: 建和元年，桓帝立勃遒亭侯便親為恢嗣，是為恭王。

translations.literal: First year of Jianhe—Emperor Huan established pavilion marquis of Boqiu Bianqin as Hui heir—this is King Gong.
