translations.idiomatic: In Jianhe 1 Emperor Huan named Bianqin of Boqiu pavilion marquis as Hui's heir—later King Gong.

sentences.zh: 立十三年薨，子孝王統嗣。

translations.literal: Established thirteen years died; son King Xiao Tong succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: He ruled thirteen years; King Xiao Tong succeeded.

sentences.zh: 立八年薨，子王赦立；

translations.literal: Established eight years died; son Wang She established;
translations.idiomatic: He ruled eight years; Wang She succeeded;

sentences.zh: 建安中薨，無子，國除。

translations.literal: In Jian'an died; no son; state abolished.
translations.idiomatic: He died in the Jian'an era without an heir; the kingdom was abolished.

sentences.zh: 廣陵思王荊

translations.literal: King Si of Guangling Jing.
translations.idiomatic: King Si of Guangling, Liu Jing

sentences.zh: 廣陵思王荊，建武十五年封山陽公，十七年進爵為王。

translations.literal: King Si of Guangling Jing—in fifteenth year of Jianwu enfeoffed Shanyang duke; seventeenth year advanced rank to king.
translations.idiomatic: King Si of Guangling was duke of Shanyang in Jianwu 15 and king in Jianwu 17.

sentences.zh: 荊性刻急隱害，[94]有才能而喜文法。

translations.literal: Jing's nature harsh urgent hidden harm; [94] had talent ability yet loved literary law.
translations.idiomatic: Jing was harsh and secretive note ninety-four in the source yet talented and fond of legal craft.

sentences.zh: 光武崩，大行在前殿，荊哭不哀，而作飛書，封以方底，[95]令蒼頭詐稱東海王彊舅大鴻臚郭況書與彊曰：「君王無罪，猥被斥廢，而兄弟至有束縛入牢獄者。

translations.literal: Guangwu collapsed—great procession at front hall—Jing wept not mournful—yet made flying letter—sealed with square bottom; [95] ordered dark head falsely claim East Sea King Qiang maternal uncle grand herald Guo Kuang letter to Qiang saying: "Lord king without guilt—undeservedly dismissed and cast aside—yet brothers even have bound enter prison.
translations.idiomatic: When Guangwu lay in state Jing showed no grief but forged a letter sealed square, sent by a slave posing as Guo Kuang to Qiang: You were innocent yet cast off—your brothers lie in chains."

sentences.zh: 太后失職，別守北宮，[96]及至年老，遠斥居邊，[97]海內深痛，觀者鼻酸。

translations.literal: Empress dowager lost duty—separately guarded Northern Palace; [96] reaching old age—far banished dwell border; [97] within seas deep pain—watchers nose sour.
translations.idiomatic: The empress dowager was ousted to the Northern Palace note ninety-six in the source—aged and banished note ninety-seven in the source—the empire weeps."

sentences.zh: 及太后屍柩在堂，洛陽吏以次捕斬賓客，至有一家三屍伏堂者，痛甚矣！

translations.literal: When empress dowager corpse coffin in hall—Luoyang clerks in sequence arrested and beheaded guests—reaching one household three corpses lying hall—pain extreme!
translations.idiomatic: While her bier stood in Luoyang officers slew her retainers—three bodies in one house—such grief!"

sentences.zh: 今天下有喪，弓弩張設甚備。

translations.literal: Now all-under-heaven has mourning—bows crossbows stretched set very complete.
translations.idiomatic: The realm is in mourning—every bow is strung."

sentences.zh: 間梁松勑虎賁史曰：『吏以便宜見非，勿有所拘，[98]封侯難再得也。

translations.literal: Among Liang Song ordered tiger guard clerk saying: 'Clerk by convenience seen fault—do not have what restrains; [98] enfeoff marquis hard again obtain.
translations.idiomatic: Liang Song told the tiger guards: Do not spare clerks who exceed duty note ninety-eight in the source—a marquisate rarely comes twice."

sentences.zh: 』郎官竊悲之，為王寒心累息。

translations.literal: ' Gentleman officials secretly grieved for them—for king chill heart piled sighs.
translations.idiomatic: Court gentlemen shivered for you."

sentences.zh: [99]今天下爭欲思刻賊王以求功，寧有量邪！

translations.literal: [99] Now all-under-heaven vies wishing think carve thief king to seek merit—could there be measure!
translations.idiomatic: [99] The empire schemes to wound you for merit—there is no limit!"

sentences.zh: 若歸並二國之觿，可聚百萬，君王為之主，鼓行無前，功易於太山破雞子，輕於四馬載鴻毛，此湯、武兵也。

translations.literal: If combine two states' forces—can gather million—lord king as them master—drum march none before—merit easier than Mount Tai breaking egg—lighter than four horses carrying goose feather—this is Tang, Wu arms.
translations.idiomatic: Merge two kingdoms and raise a million under your banner—victory easier than smashing an egg on Tai—lighter than a goose feather in a chariot—true Tang-Wu warfare."

sentences.zh: 今年軒轅星有白氣，星家及喜事者，[100]皆云白氣者喪，軒轅女主之位。

translations.literal: This year Xuanyuan star has white qi—star masters and joy-affair persons; [100] all say white qi means mourning—Xuanyuan chief woman's position.
translations.idiomatic: This year white vapor marks Xuanyuan note one hundred in the source—omen of mourning for the ruling lady."

sentences.zh: 又太白前出西方，至午兵當起。

translations.literal: Also Great White before emerged west—reaching noon weapons ought rise.
translations.idiomatic: Great White advances from the west—by noon arms will stir."

sentences.zh: [101]又太子星色黑，至辰日輒變赤。

translations.literal: [101] Also heir apparent star color black—reaching chen day always changes red.
translations.idiomatic: [101] The heir star is black—on chen days it turns red."

sentences.zh: [102]夫黑為病，赤為兵，王努力卒事。

translations.literal: [102] As for black is sickness—red is weapons—king strive finish affair.
translations.idiomatic: [102] Black means plague, red means war—finish the deed."

sentences.zh: 高祖起亭長，陛下興白水，何況於王陛下長子，故副主哉！

translations.literal: High Ancestor rose post chief—Your Majesty rises White Water—how much more king Your Majesty eldest son—former deputy lord!
translations.idiomatic: Gaozu was a village head—you rose from White Water—how much more you, eldest son and heir!"

sentences.zh: 上以求天下事必舉，下以雪除沉沒之恥，報死母之讎。

translations.literal: Above to seek all-under-heaven affair must rise—below to wipe remove sunk shame—avenge dead mother's enemy.
translations.idiomatic: Strike high to master the realm and low to wash away shame and avenge your mother."

sentences.zh: 精誠所加，金石為開。

translations.literal: Pure sincerity what applies—metal stone thereby opens.
translations.idiomatic: Wholehearted resolve splits metal and stone."

sentences.zh: [103]當為秋霜，無為檻羊。

translations.literal: [103] Ought be autumn frost—not be penned sheep.
translations.idiomatic: [103] Be autumn frost on the flock—not a sheep in the pen."

sentences.zh: [104]雖欲為檻羊，又可得乎！

translations.literal: [104] Although wish be penned sheep—again can obtain!
translations.idiomatic: [104] Even as penned sheep you cannot escape!"

sentences.zh: 竊見諸相工言王貴，天子法也。

translations.literal: I privately see various physiognomists say king noble—Son of Heaven pattern.
translations.idiomatic: Physiognomists say your face bears the imperial pattern."

sentences.zh: 人主崩亡，閭閻之伍尚為盜賊，欲有所望，何況王邪！

translations.literal: Ruler lord collapses dies—lane-alley ranks still become bandits—wish have what hope—how much more king!
translations.idiomatic: When the emperor dies common thieves stake claims—how much more a prince!"

sentences.zh: 夫受命之君，天之所立，不可謀也。

translations.literal: As for receiving mandate's lord—heaven what establishes—cannot plot.
translations.idiomatic: Heaven sets the legitimate ruler—plotting cannot touch him."

sentences.zh: 今新帝人之所置，強者為右。

translations.literal: Now new emperor people what established—strong one is right.
translations.idiomatic: The new emperor is whoever seizes power—the strong lead."

sentences.zh: 願君王為高祖、陛下所志，[105]無為扶蘇、將閭叫呼天也。

translations.literal: Wish lord king act High Ancestor, Your Majesty what aspired; [105] not act Fusu, Jianglu crying heaven.
translations.idiomatic: Match Gaozu and your father's will note one hundred five in the source—do not die like Fusu or Jianglu crying to heaven."

sentences.zh: 」[106]強得書惶怖，即執其使，封書上之。

translations.literal: " [106] Qiang obtained letter fearful—immediately seized his envoy—sealed letter submitted upward.
translations.idiomatic: [106] Qiang seized the courier and forwarded the sealed letter."

sentences.zh: 顯宗以荊母弟，秘其事，遣荊出止河南宮。

translations.literal: Emperor Ming because Jing mother's younger brother—secret his affair—dispatched Jing exit stop Henan palace.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Ming hid the affair as fraternal kindness and lodged Jing in the Henan palace.

sentences.zh: 時西羌反，荊不得志，冀天下因羌驚動有變，私迎能為星者與謀議。

translations.literal: At time Western Qiang rebelled—Jing did not obtain intent—hoped all-under-heaven because Qiang alarm move have change—privately welcomed able make stars persons with plot deliberation.
translations.idiomatic: When the western Qiang rebelled Jing hoped for turmoil and secretly consulted astrologers.

sentences.zh: 帝聞之，乃徙封荊廣陵王，遣之國。

translations.literal: Emperor heard it—then transferred enfeoff Jing Guangling king—sent him state.
translations.idiomatic: Learning this the emperor made him king of Guangling and sent him to his fief.

sentences.zh: 其後荊復呼相工謂曰：「我貌類先帝。

translations.literal: Afterward Jing again summoned physiognomist saying: "My appearance resembles former emperor.
translations.idiomatic: Later Jing asked a physiognomist: "I look like my father."

sentences.zh: 先帝三十得天下，我今亦三十，可起兵未？

translations.literal: Former emperor thirty obtained all-under-heaven—I now also thirty—can raise troops yet?
translations.idiomatic: Father took the realm at thirty—I am thirty—may I rise?"

sentences.zh: 」相者詣吏告之，荊惶恐，自繫獄。

translations.literal: " Physiognomist went clerks reported him—Jing fearful—himself bound prison.
translations.idiomatic: The man reported him; Jing surrendered to jail.

sentences.zh: 帝復加恩，不考極其事，下詔不得臣屬吏人，唯食租如故，使相、中尉謹宿衛之。

translations.literal: Emperor again added grace—did not investigate utmost affair—issued edict not permitted minister-subordinate clerks people—only eat rent as before—ordered chancellor and commandant of capital guards carefully night guard him.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor spared him full trial: no officials might serve him—only rent—and the chancellor and commandant guarded him.

sentences.zh: 荊猶不改。

translations.literal: Jing still did not reform.
translations.idiomatic: Jing did not mend his ways.

sentences.zh: 其後使巫祭祀祝詛，有司舉奏，請誅之，荊自殺。

translations.literal: Afterward ordered shamans sacrifice prayer curse—authorities memorialized requesting execute him—Jing killed himself.
translations.idiomatic: Later he sent witches to curse; the ministry demanded execution—Jing killed himself.

sentences.zh: 立二十九年死。

translations.literal: Established twenty-nine years died.
translations.idiomatic: He died in the twenty-ninth year of his reign.

sentences.zh: 帝憐傷之，賜謚曰思王。

translations.literal: Emperor pitied wounded him—granted posthumous title King Si.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor mourned him with the posthumous title Si.

sentences.zh: 十四年，封荊子元壽為廣陵侯，服王璽綬，食荊故國六縣；

translations.literal: Fourteenth year—enfeoffed Jing's son Yuanshou as Guangling marquis—wore king seals ribbons—ate Jing's former state six counties;
translations.idiomatic: In year 14 Yuanshou became marquis of Guangling with princely seals and six counties of Jing's old realm;

sentences.zh: 又封元壽弟三人為鄉侯。

translations.literal: Again enfeoffed Yuanshou's three younger brothers as village marquises.
translations.idiomatic: three of Yuanshou's brothers became village marquises.

sentences.zh: 明年，帝東巡狩，徵元壽兄弟會東平宮，班賜御服器物，又取皇子輿馬，悉以與之。

translations.literal: Next year, emperor east toured hunt—summoned Yuanshou brothers assemble East Peace palace—distributed gifts imperial garments vessels—also took imperial sons carriages horses—all gave them.
translations.idiomatic: The next year the emperor toured east, summoned Yuanshou and brothers to the East Peace palace, gave imperial wardrobe and princes' carriages and horses.

sentences.zh: 建初七年，肅宗詔元壽兄弟與諸王俱朝京師。

translations.literal: Seventh year of Jianchu—Emperor Zhang edicted Yuanshou brothers with various kings together court capital.
translations.idiomatic: In Jianchu 7 Emperor Zhang summoned Yuanshou and brothers with the other kings to court.

sentences.zh: 元壽卒，子商嗣。

translations.literal: Yuanshou died; son Shang succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: Yuanshou died; Shang succeeded.

sentences.zh: 商卒，子條嗣，傳國於後。

translations.literal: Shang died; son Tiao succeeded—transmitted state to posterity.
translations.idiomatic: Shang died; Tiao inherited and passed the title down.

sentences.zh: 臨淮懷公衡

translations.literal: Duke Huai of Linhuai Heng.
translations.idiomatic: Duke Huai of Linhuai, Liu Heng

sentences.zh: 臨淮懷公衡，建武十五年立，未及進爵為王而薨，無子，國除。

translations.literal: Duke Huai of Linhuai Heng—in fifteenth year of Jianwu established—before reaching advance rank to king then died—no son—state abolished.
translations.idiomatic: Duke Huai of Linhuai was installed in Jianwu 15 but died before promotion to king without an heir—the kingdom lapsed.

sentences.zh: 中山簡王焉

translations.literal: King Jian of Zhongshan Yan.
translations.idiomatic: King Jian of Zhongshan, Liu Yan

sentences.zh: 中山簡王焉，建武十五年封左*

translations.literal: King Jian of Zhongshan Yan—in fifteenth year of Jianwu enfeoffed Left —
translations.idiomatic: King Jian of Zhongshan in Jianwu 15 was enfeoffed duke of Left—

translations.literal: (Feng)
translations.idiomatic: Feng—

sentences.zh: *翊公，十七年進爵為王。

translations.literal: —Yi duke—seventeenth year advanced rank to king.
translations.idiomatic: —Fengyi duke—and king in Jianwu 17.

sentences.zh: 焉以郭太后少子故，獨留京師。

translations.literal: Yan because Empress Guo youngest son reason—alone remained capital.
translations.idiomatic: As Empress Guo's youngest son Yan alone stayed at court.

sentences.zh: 三十年，徙封中山王。

translations.literal: Thirtieth year—transferred enfeoff Zhongshan king.
translations.idiomatic: In Jianwu 30 he became king of Zhongshan.

sentences.zh: 永平二年冬，諸王來會辟雍，事畢歸蕃，詔焉與俱就國，從以虎賁官騎。

translations.literal: Second year of Yongping winter—various kings came assemble Biyong—affair finished return feudatories—edict Yan with together go state—followed by tiger guard official riders.
translations.idiomatic: In Yongping 2 winter the kings met at Biyong; when they left for their fiefs Yan was ordered with them and given tiger guard escort.

sentences.zh: [107]焉上疏辭讓，顯宗報曰：「凡諸侯出境，必備左右，故夾谷之會，司馬以從。

translations.literal: [107] Yan submitted memorial declining—Emperor Ming replied saying: "All feudal lords exit border—must prepare left right—therefore Jiagu meeting—marshal followed.
translations.idiomatic: [107] Yan declined; Emperor Ming answered: "Princes leaving the capital need escort—at Jiagu the marshal attended."

sentences.zh: [108]今五國各官騎百人，稱娖前行，[109]皆北軍胡騎，便兵善射，弓不空發，中必決眥。

translations.literal: [108] Now five states each official riders hundred persons—called orderly advance front; [109] all northern army Hu riders—handy arms good shoot—bow not empty release—hit must burst eye corner.
translations.idiomatic: [108] Each of five kingdoms gets a hundred riders note one hundred nine in the source—northern Hu cavalry who never miss."

sentences.zh: [110]夫有文事必有武備，所以重蕃職也。

translations.literal: [110] When there are civil affairs there must be martial preparation—thereby emphasize feudal duty.
translations.idiomatic: [110] Civil rule needs armed escort—that dignifies a prince."

sentences.zh: 王其勿辭。

translations.literal: King—do not decline.
translations.idiomatic: Do not refuse."

sentences.zh: 」帝以焉郭太后偏愛，特加恩寵，獨得往來京師。

translations.literal: " Emperor because Yan Empress Guo partial love—specially added grace favor—alone obtained go-come capital.
translations.idiomatic: " Because Empress Guo favored Yan he alone could travel to and from the capital.

sentences.zh: 十五年，焉姬韓序有過，焉縊殺之，國相舉奏，坐削安險縣。

translations.literal: Fifteenth year—Yan's concubine Han Xu had fault—Yan strangled killed her—state chancellor memorialized—sat guilty stripped Anxian county.
translations.idiomatic: In year 15 Yan strangled a concubine named Han Xu; the chancellor reported him and Anxian county was stripped.

sentences.zh: [111]元和中，肅宗復以安險還中山。

translations.literal: [111] In Yuanhe period—Emperor Zhang again returned Anxian to Zhongshan.
translations.idiomatic: [111] In Yuanhe Emperor Zhang restored Anxian to Zhongshan.

sentences.zh: 立五十二年，永元二年薨。

translations.literal: Established fifty-two years—second year of Yongyuan died.
translations.idiomatic: He ruled fifty-two years and died in Yongyuan 2.

sentences.zh: 自中興至和帝時，皇子始封薨者，皆賻錢三千萬，布三萬疋；

translations.literal: From restoration to Emperor He's time—imperial sons first enfeoff died—all burial cash thirty million; cloth thirty thousand bolts;
translations.idiomatic: Since the restoration a prince who died on first enfeoffment received thirty million cash and thirty thousand bolts;

sentences.zh: 嗣王薨，賻錢千萬、布萬疋。

translations.literal: Heir king died—burial cash ten million; cloth ten thousand bolts.
translations.idiomatic: a successor prince ten million and ten thousand bolts.

sentences.zh: 是時竇太后臨朝，竇憲兄弟擅權，太后及憲等，東海出也，[112]故睦於焉而重於禮，加賻錢一億。

translations.literal: At this time Empress Dowager Dou faced court—Dou Xian brothers monopolized power—empress dowager and Xian and others—East Sea born; [112] therefore friendly to Yan and heavy in ritual—added burial cash one hundred million.
translations.idiomatic: Empress Dowager Dou and her kin favored Yan note one hundred twelve in the source and added one hundred million cash.

sentences.zh: 詔濟南、東海二王皆會。

translations.literal: Edict Jinan, East Sea two kings all assemble.
translations.idiomatic: The kings of Jinan and the East Sea were summoned to the funeral.

sentences.zh: 大為修頤塋，開神道，[113]平夷吏人頤墓以千數，作者萬餘人。

translations.literal: Greatly repaired Yi mound—opened spirit road; [113] leveled commoners Yi tombs by thousands—workers over ten thousand.
translations.idiomatic: They raised a vast tomb and spirit path note one hundred thirteen in the source—leveling thousands of common graves—over ten thousand laborers.

sentences.zh: 發常山、鉅鹿、涿郡柏黃腸雜木，[114]三郡不能備，復調餘州郡工徒及送致者數千人。

translations.literal: Mobilized Changshan, Julu, Zhuo commanderies cypress yellow-liner mixed woods; [114] three commanderies could not supply—again drafted remaining province commanderies work gangs and deliverers several thousand persons.
translations.idiomatic: They took cypress from Changshan, Julu, and Zhuo note one hundred fourteen in the source—when three commanderies fell short other provinces sent thousands more.

sentences.zh: 凡征發搖動六州十八郡，制度餘國莫及。

translations.literal: As for levy dispatch shook six provinces eighteen counties—system degree remaining kingdoms none reach.
translations.idiomatic: The draft stirred six provinces and eighteen commanderies—no other funeral matched it.

sentences.zh: 子夷王憲嗣。

translations.literal: Son King Yi Xian succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: King Yi Xian succeeded.

sentences.zh: 永元四年，封憲弟十一人為列侯。

translations.literal: Fourth year of Yongyuan—Xian's eleven younger brothers enfeoffed full marquises.
translations.idiomatic: In Yongyuan 4 eleven of Xian's brothers became full marquises.

sentences.zh: 憲立二十二年薨，子孝王弘嗣。

translations.literal: Xian established twenty-two years died; son King Xiao Hong succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: Xian ruled twenty-two years; King Xiao Hong succeeded.

sentences.zh: 永寧元年，封弘二弟為亭侯。

translations.literal: First year of Yongning—Hong's two younger brothers enfeoffed pavilion marquises.
translations.idiomatic: In Yongning 1 two of Hong's brothers became pavilion marquises.

sentences.zh: 弘立二十八年薨，子穆王暢嗣。

translations.literal: Hong established twenty-eight years died; son King Mu Chang succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: Hong ruled twenty-eight years; King Mu Chang succeeded.

sentences.zh: 永和六年，封暢弟荊為南鄉侯。

translations.literal: Sixth year of Yonghe—Chang's younger brother Jing enfeoffed Marquis of Nanxiang.
translations.idiomatic: In Yonghe 6 Chang's brother Jing became marquis of Nanxiang.

sentences.zh: 暢立三十四年薨，子節王稚嗣，無子，國除。

translations.literal: Chang established thirty-four years died; son King Jie Zhi succeeded—no son—state abolished.
translations.idiomatic: Chang ruled thirty-four years; King Jie Zhi died without an heir and the kingdom was abolished.

sentences.zh: 琅邪孝王京

translations.literal: King Xiao of Langye Jing.
translations.idiomatic: King Xiao of Langye, Liu Jing

sentences.zh: 琅邪孝王京，建武十五年封琅邪公，十七年進爵為王。

translations.literal: King Xiao of Langye, Liu Jing—in Jianwu 15 he was enfeoffed as duke of Langye, and in the seventeenth year he was advanced in rank to king.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Jing, the Filial King of Langye, was first enfeoffed as duke of Langye in Jianwu 15; two years later he was raised to the rank of king.

sentences.zh: 京性恭孝，好經學，顯宗尤愛幸，賞賜恩寵殊異，莫與為比。

translations.literal: Jing by nature was reverent and filial; he loved classical learning; Emperor Ming favored him above others in affection and reward—his gifts and favor were extraordinary; none could compare.
translations.idiomatic: Jing was humble and dutiful, steeped in the Classics; Emperor Ming doted on him and lavished honors no other prince could match.

sentences.zh: 永平二年，以太山之蓋、南武陽、華，[115]東萊之昌陽、盧鄉、東牟六縣益琅邪。

translations.literal: Yongping 2—from Mount Tai they took Gai, Nanwuyang, and Hua; [115] from Donglai they took Changyang, Luxiang, and Dongmou—six counties in all—to augment Langye.
translations.idiomatic: In Yongping 2 six counties—Gai, Nanwuyang, and Hua from Mount Tai, and Changyang, Luxiang, and Dongmou from Donglai—were added to Langye.

sentences.zh: [116]五年，乃就國。

translations.literal: [116] In the fifth year he then went to his fief.
translations.idiomatic: [116] In the fifth year he finally took up residence in his kingdom.

sentences.zh: 光烈皇后崩，帝悉以太后遺金寶財物賜京。

translations.literal: When Empress Guanglie died, the emperor gave Jing all the gold, jewels, and goods the late empress dowager had left behind.
translations.idiomatic: After Empress Guanglie passed away, the emperor turned over to Jing the entire legacy of gold, gems, and goods she had left.

sentences.zh: 京都莒，好修宮室，窮極伎巧，殿館壁帶皆飾以金銀。

translations.literal: Jing made his capital at Ju; he loved to build palaces and halls, exhausting every craft and skill; the galleries and belt-courses of his halls were all ornamented with gold and silver.
translations.idiomatic: With his seat at Ju he poured wealth into palaces, pushing craft to its limits until galleries and friezes gleamed with gold and silver.

sentences.zh: [117]數上詩賦頌德，帝嘉美，下之史官。

translations.literal: [117] Repeatedly he presented poems and rhapsodies praising virtue; the emperor praised them and had them filed with the historiographers.
translations.idiomatic: [117] He repeatedly submitted verse and fu praising the court; the emperor commended the pieces and ordered them preserved among the official histories.

sentences.zh: 京國中有城陽景王祠，吏人奉祠。

translations.literal: Within Jing's kingdom was a shrine to King Jing of Chengyang; officials and commoners worshipped there.
translations.idiomatic: His realm housed a temple to King Jing of Chengyang, where clerks and townspeople kept up the cult.

sentences.zh: 神數下言，宮中多不便利，京上書願徙宮開陽，以華、蓋、南武陽、厚丘、贛榆五縣[118]易東海之開陽、臨沂，肅宗許之。

translations.literal: The spirit repeatedly sent down words that life in the palace was largely inconvenient; Jing memorialized, wishing to move his seat to Kaiyang, trading Hua, Gai, Nanwuyang, Houqiu, and Ganyu—five counties [118]—for Kaiyang and Linyi in Donghai; Emperor Zhang granted it.
translations.idiomatic: Oracles kept insisting the palace site was inauspicious, so Jing asked to shift his capital to Kaiyang, trading five counties—Hua, Gai, Nanwuyang, Houqiu, and Ganyu—for Kaiyang and Linyi in Donghai. Emperor Zhang approved.

sentences.zh: 立三十一年薨，葬東海即丘廣平亭，有詔割亭屬開陽。

translations.literal: He had been established thirty-one years when he died; he was buried at Guangping Pavilion in Jiuqiu, Donghai; an edict carved out the pavilion precinct and attached it to Kaiyang.
translations.idiomatic: After thirty-one years on the throne he died and was interred at Guangping Pavilion in Jiuqiu, Donghai; an edict transferred that pavilion district to Kaiyang.

translations.literal: Editorial mark one hundred nineteen in the received text.
translations.idiomatic: Manuscript note [119].

sentences.zh: 子夷王宇嗣。

translations.literal: His son, King Yi Yu, succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: His son, King Yi of Langye, Liu Yu, inherited the title.

sentences.zh: 建初七年，封宇弟十三人為列侯。

translations.literal: Jianchu 7—thirteen of Yu's younger brothers were enfeoffed as full marquises.
translations.idiomatic: In Jianchu 7 thirteen of Yu's brothers were made full marquises.

sentences.zh: 元和元年，封孝王孫二人為列侯。

translations.literal: Yuanhe 1—two grandsons of the Filial King were enfeoffed as full marquises.
translations.idiomatic: In Yuanhe 1 two of the late Filial King's grandsons were given full marquisates.

sentences.zh: 宇立二十年薨，子恭王壽嗣。

translations.literal: Yu had been established twenty years when he died; his son, King Gong Shou, succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: Yu ruled twenty years; King Gong Liu Shou succeeded him.

sentences.zh: 永初元年，封壽弟八人為列侯。

translations.literal: Yongchu 1—eight of Shou's younger brothers were enfeoffed as full marquises.
translations.idiomatic: In Yongchu 1 eight of Shou's brothers became full marquises.

sentences.zh: 立十七年薨，子貞王尊嗣。

translations.literal: He had been established seventeen years when he died; his son, King Zhen Zun, succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: After seventeen years he died; King Zhen Liu Zun succeeded.

sentences.zh: 延光二年，封尊弟四人為鄉侯。

translations.literal: Yanguang 2—four of Zun's younger brothers were enfeoffed as district marquises.
translations.idiomatic: In Yanguang 2 four of Zun's brothers were made district marquises.

sentences.zh: 尊立十八年薨，子安王據嗣。

translations.literal: Zun had been established eighteen years when he died; his son, King An Ju, succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: Zun ruled eighteen years; King An Liu Ju followed.

sentences.zh: 永和五年，封據弟三人為鄉侯。

translations.literal: Yonghe 5—three of Ju's younger brothers were enfeoffed as district marquises.
translations.idiomatic: In Yonghe 5 three of Ju's brothers received district marquisates.

sentences.zh: 據立四十七年薨，子順王容嗣。

translations.literal: Ju had been established forty-seven years when he died; his son, King Shun Rong, succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: Ju held the throne forty-seven years; King Shun Liu Rong succeeded.

sentences.zh: 初平元年，遣弟邈至長安奉章貢獻，帝以邈為九江太守，封陽都侯。

translations.literal: Chuping 1—he sent his younger brother Miao to Chang'an with memorials and tribute; the emperor appointed Miao governor of Jiujiang and enfeoffed him as marquis of Yangdu.
translations.idiomatic: In Chuping 1 he dispatched his brother Miao to Chang'an with memorials and tribute; the emperor named Miao governor of Jiujiang and marquis of Yangdu.

translations.literal: Editorial mark one hundred twenty in the received text.
translations.idiomatic: Manuscript note [120].

sentences.zh: 容立八年薨，國絕。

translations.literal: Rong had been established eight years when he died; the state was cut off.
translations.idiomatic: Rong ruled eight years; with his death the line lapsed and the fief was abolished.

sentences.zh: 初，邈至長安，盛稱東郡太守曹操忠誠於帝，操以此德於邈。

translations.literal: Earlier, when Miao reached Chang'an, he extravagantly praised Cao Cao, governor of Dong commandery, for his loyalty to the emperor; for this Cao Cao felt grateful to Miao.
translations.idiomatic: When Miao first reached Chang'an he praised Cao Cao, governor of Dong, as loyal to the throne, and Cao Cao repaid that kindness.

sentences.zh: 建安十一年，復立容子熙為王。

translations.literal: Jian'an 11—Rong's son Xi was re-established as king.
translations.idiomatic: In Jian'an 11 Rong's son Xi was restored to the kingship.

sentences.zh: 在位十一年，坐謀欲過江，被誅，國除。

translations.literal: He held the position eleven years; for plotting to cross the southward river he was executed, and the state was removed.
translations.idiomatic: He ruled eleven years, then was executed for plotting to flee south of the river; the kingdom was abolished.

translations.literal: Appraisal heading.
translations.idiomatic: Appraisal.

sentences.zh: 贊曰：光武十子，胙土分王。

translations.literal: The appraisal states: Guangwu had ten sons; they received land apportioned and were invested as kings.
translations.idiomatic: The appraisal runs: Guangwu's ten sons received fiefs and ruled as kings.

sentences.zh: 沛獻尊節，楚英流放。

translations.literal: Pei Xian upheld integrity; Chu Ying was banished.
translations.idiomatic: The Duke of Pei kept his integrity; the Prince of Chu was cast away.

sentences.zh: [121]延既怨詛，荊亦觖望。

translations.literal: [121] Yan having nursed resentment and curses, Jing likewise harbored frustrated ambition.
translations.idiomatic: [121] Yan muttered curses in bitterness; Jing too nursed thwarted hopes.

sentences.zh: 濟南陰謀，琅邪驕宕。

translations.literal: Jinan plotted in secret; Langye grew proud and dissolute.
translations.idiomatic: Jinan schemed in the shadows; Langye turned extravagant and proud.

sentences.zh: 中山、臨淮，無聞夭喪。

translations.literal: Zhongshan and Linhuai—without renown, cut off in youth.
translations.idiomatic: Zhongshan and Linhuai passed young and left little mark.

sentences.zh: [122]東平好善，辭中委相。

translations.literal: [122] Dongping loved the good; from office he yielded the chancellorship.
translations.idiomatic: [122] Dongping pursued virtue and resigned the prime ministership mid-career.

sentences.zh: 謙謙恭王，寔惟三讓。

translations.literal: Humble, humble the Reverent King—truly thrice he yielded.
translations.idiomatic: The Reverent King of humility thrice declined high office.

sentences.zh: 【校勘記】

translations.literal: Textual collation notes.
translations.idiomatic: Collation notes.

sentences.zh: 一四二三頁六行 立母郭氏為*[皇]*后 集解引沉欽韓說，謂案文少一「皇」字。

translations.literal: Page 1423, line 6: 'established his mother the Guo as [empress]'—the Jijie quotes Shen Qinhan, who says the text lacks one character 皇 (imperial).
translations.idiomatic: p. 1423, l. 6, on enfeoffing Lady Guo as empress: Shen Qinhan notes the received text is missing 皇 (imperial).

sentences.zh: 今據補。

translations.literal: Now according to this it is supplied.
translations.idiomatic: The character is supplied accordingly.

sentences.zh: 一四二四頁一二行 使*

translations.literal: Page 1424, line 12: 'ordered*'—text breaks off.
translations.idiomatic: p. 1424, l. 12: manuscript lacuna after 使 (ordered).

translations.literal: (Grand)
translations.idiomatic: Variant gloss 'Grand'—some editions insert it before the minister of works in the following clause.

sentences.zh: *司空持節護喪事 據集解引錢大昕說刪。

translations.literal: *superintendent of works bearing credentials overseeing the funeral'—according to the Jijie citing Qian Daxin, deleted.
translations.idiomatic: The clause '*minister of works with credentials for the funeral' is struck per Qian Daxin in the Jijie.

sentences.zh: 按：袁紀作「司空魴」，無「大」字。

translations.literal: Comment: the Yuan ji reads 'superintendent of works Fang,' without the character 大.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Yuan ji has 'minister Fang' without 大 (grand).

sentences.zh: 一四二四頁一四行 比陽公主 按：校補引柳從辰說，謂「比」讀為「沘」。

translations.literal: Page 1424, line 14: Princess of Biyang—comment: the Jiaobu cites Liu Congchen, who says 比 is read as 沘.
translations.idiomatic: p. 1424, l. 14, Princess of Biyang: Liu Congchen reads 比 as the river name Bi (沘).

sentences.zh: 一四二五頁一二行 彊立十八年 按：校補引柳從辰說，謂「八」疑「六」之鬥。

translations.literal: Page 1425, line 12: 'Qiang established eighteen years'—comment: the Jiaobu cites Liu Congchen, who says 八 may be a corruption of 六.
translations.idiomatic: p. 1425, l. 12, 'ruled eighteen years': Liu Congchen suspects 八 (eight) is a misprint for 六 (six).

sentences.zh: 黃山謂此從郭后十七年被廢追數之，乃史之誤。

translations.literal: Huang Shan says this counts back from the seventeenth year when Empress Guo was deposed—it is the historian's error.
translations.idiomatic: Huang Shan argues the figure counts from Empress Guo's deposition in year 17 and reflects a scribal mistake.

sentences.zh: 一四二七頁二行 封右*

translations.literal: Page 1427, line 2: 'enfeoffed as right*'—text breaks off.
translations.idiomatic: p. 1427, l. 2: lacuna after 'right.'

translations.literal: (Feng)
translations.idiomatic: Interlinear gloss 'Feng' for the place-name fragment.

sentences.zh: *翊公 刊誤謂衍「馮」字。

translations.literal: *yi duke'—the Kanwu says the character 馮 is redundant.
translations.idiomatic: Kanwu holds 馮 is a superfluous graph in '*Yi duke.'

sentences.zh: 集解引錢大昕說，謂中山王焉傳「封左馮翊公」，與此傳同，皆衍文也。

translations.literal: The Jijie cites Qian Daxin, who says the biography of King Yan of Zhongshan reads 'enfeoffed as duke of Left Fengyi'—the same as here—they are both redundant text.
translations.idiomatic: Qian Daxin (Jijie) notes King Yan's biography has the same inflated 'Left Fengyi duke' phrasing—both are gloss creep.

sentences.zh: 左翊、右翊蓋取嘉名，非分馮翊地為左右。

translations.literal: Left Yi and Right Yi are pleasing titles to take—it is not that Fengyi territory was split into left and right.
translations.idiomatic: 'Left' and 'Right Yi' are honorific labels, not a partition of Fengyi commandery.

sentences.zh: 今據刪。

translations.literal: Now according to this it is deleted.
translations.idiomatic: The spurious 馮 is dropped accordingly.

sentences.zh: 一四二七頁六行 封輔子寶為沛侯 按：集解引錢大昕說，謂沛為王國之名，不應更有「沛侯」，疑字有鬥。

translations.literal: Page 1427, line 6: 'enfeoffed Fu's son Bao as marquis of Pei'—comment: the Jijie cites Qian Daxin, who says Pei is already a royal domain name; there should not also be a 'marquis of Pei'—the characters are suspect.
translations.idiomatic: p. 1427, l. 6: Qian Daxin doubts 'marquis of Pei' because Pei was a kingdom title—likely corrupt wording.

sentences.zh: 一四二八頁一一行 歡喜大恩 按：汲本、集解本「大」作「天」。

translations.literal: Page 1428, line 11: 'rejoice in the great grace'—comment: the Ji edition and Jijie edition read 大 as 天 (heaven).
translations.idiomatic: p. 1428, l. 11: Ji and Jijie texts have 天 (heaven's) for 大 (great).

sentences.zh: 一四二八頁一二行 尚浮屠之仁祠 按：通鑒「祠」作「慈」。

translations.literal: Page 1428, line 12: 'esteemed the Buddha's benevolent shrine'—comment: the Comprehensive Mirror reads 祠 as 慈 (compassion).
translations.idiomatic: p. 1428, l. 12: Zizhi tongjian has 慈 (compassion) for 祠 (shrine).

sentences.zh: 一四二九頁一〇行 使伎人奴婢*

translations.literal: Page 1429, line 10: 'ordered performers and slaves*'—text breaks off.
translations.idiomatic: p. 1429, l. 10: lacuna after 'performers and slaves.'

sentences.zh: (妓士)

translations.literal: (female performers)
translations.idiomatic: Alternate gloss 'female performers' joining the previous broken clause.

sentences.zh: **[工技]*鼓吹悉從 據汲本改。

translations.literal: **[craftsmen]* with music and drums all followed'—according to the Ji edition, emended.
translations.idiomatic: Emended per Ji edition: **[craftsmen]* and full bands accompanied him.'

sentences.zh: 按：刊誤謂「妓士」當作「工技」，梁節王傳中亦有工技也。

translations.literal: Comment: the Kanwu says 妓士 should read 工技 (craftsmen); the biography of King Jie of Liang also has 工技.
translations.idiomatic: Kanwu: read 妓士 as 工技; Liang Jie's biography uses the same term.

sentences.zh: 一四三〇頁七行 肅宗封英子*[種]*楚侯*

translations.literal: Page 1430, line 7: 'Emperor Zhang enfeoffed Ying's son *[Zhong]* as marquis of Chu*'—lacunae marked.
translations.idiomatic: p. 1430, l. 7: broken text on enfeoffing Ying's son Zhong as marquis of Chu.

translations.literal: (Zhong)
translations.idiomatic: Gloss: the personal name Zhong.

sentences.zh: * 集解引錢大昕說，謂當云「封英子種楚侯」，傳寫顛倒耳。

translations.literal: * The Jijie cites Qian Daxin, who says it ought to read 'enfeoffed Ying's son Zhong as marquis of Chu'—copyists reversed the order.
translations.idiomatic: Qian Daxin: the phrase should be 'enfeoffed Ying's son Zhong as marquis of Chu'—scribal transposition.

sentences.zh: 今據改。

translations.literal: Now according to this it is emended.
translations.idiomatic: The text is emended accordingly.

sentences.zh: 一四三〇頁一一行 青蓋金華藻 按：校補謂續志「藻」作「蚤」，蚤通爪，爪又通瑵，本謂車蓋上琱飾彩藻，故又可作「藻」也。

translations.literal: Page 1430, line 11: green canopy and gold flowering ornament—comment: the Jiaobu says the Xu Han zhi reads 藻 as 蚤; 蚤 connects to claw; claw connects to jade fret—the reference is carved ornament on the carriage canopy, so 藻 is also acceptable.
translations.idiomatic: p. 1430, l. 11: Xu Han zhi uses 蚤 for 藻; editors explain the variant as carved canopy finial, so both readings work.

sentences.zh: 一四三一頁二行 隰陰 按：集解引惠棟說，謂本志及宗俱碑作「濕陰」，前書志作「漯陰」。

translations.literal: Page 1431, line 2: Xiyin—comment: the Jijie cites Hui Dong, who says this gazetteer and the Zongju stele read 濕陰; the Former Han treatise reads 漯陰.
translations.idiomatic: p. 1431, l. 2, Xiyin: Hui Dong notes 濕陰 in the treatise and stele, 漯陰 in Hanshu.

sentences.zh: 又引錢大昕說，謂「隰」蓋「漯」之鬥。

translations.literal: It also cites Qian Daxin, who says 隰 is probably a corruption of 漯.
translations.idiomatic: Qian Daxin adds that 隰 likely miswrites 漯.

sentences.zh: 一四三一頁一二行 惑亂和氣 按：汲本、殿本「惑」作「感」。

translations.literal: Page 1431, line 12: 'confuse and disturb harmonious qi'—comment: the Ji edition and palace edition read 惑 as 感.
translations.idiomatic: p. 1431, l. 12: Ji and palace editions have 感 (stir) for 惑 (bewilder).

sentences.zh: 一四三二頁一〇行 顧夷*

translations.literal: Page 1432, line 10: 'Gu Yi*'—text breaks off.
translations.idiomatic: p. 1432, l. 10: lacuna after Gu Yi.

translations.literal: (Wu)
translations.idiomatic: Marginal gloss 'Wu'—corrupt for 吳 in the title below.

sentences.zh: **[吳]*地記云 集解引惠棟說，謂此顧夷所撰吳地記也，「吳」訛「吾」。

translations.literal: **[Wu]*di ji says'—the Jijie cites Hui Dong, who says this is Gu Yi's Record of the Wu Region; 吳 was corrupted to 吾.
translations.idiomatic: Hui Dong (Jijie): Gu Yi's Wu di ji—吳 was miswritten as 吾.

sentences.zh: 今據改。

translations.literal: Now according to this it is emended.
translations.idiomatic: 吳 is restored accordingly.

sentences.zh: 一四三二頁一三行 鼓吹妓女宋閏 按：「妓」字當作「伎」，各本皆未正。

translations.literal: Page 1432, line 13: 'music-suite courtesan Song Run'—comment: the character 妓 should be 伎 (performer); no edition has corrected it.
translations.idiomatic: p. 1432, l. 13: read 妓 as 伎 (musician)—still uncorrected in most prints.

sentences.zh: 參閱梁冀傳校記。

translations.literal: Refer to the collation notes in the biography of Liang Ji.
translations.idiomatic: See the Liang Ji biography collation.

sentences.zh: 一四三二頁一四行 永元十一年封錯弟七人為列侯 按：汲本作「十二年」。

translations.literal: Page 1432, line 14: 'Yongyuan 11 enfeoffed seven younger brothers of Cuo as full marquises'—comment: the Ji edition reads 'twelfth year.'
translations.idiomatic: p. 1432, l. 14: Ji edition has Yongyuan 12 instead of 11.

sentences.zh: 一四三三頁七行 要帶八圍 汲本、殿本作「十圍」。

translations.literal: Page 1433, line 7: 'his belt spanned eight arm-spans'—the Ji edition and palace edition read 'ten spans.'
translations.idiomatic: p. 1433, l. 7: Ji and palace texts have ten spans around the waist instead of eight.

sentences.zh: 今按：御覽三七一、三七八引，並作「八圍」，疑作「十圍」者誤也。

translations.literal: Commentator's note: the Imperial Digest at scrolls 371 and 378 both quote 'eight spans'; I suspect the reading 'ten spans' is wrong.
translations.idiomatic: Imperial Digest 371 and 378 both quote eight spans—ten is probably the error.

sentences.zh: 一四三三頁一〇行 山陽之南平陽*

translations.literal: Page 1433, line 10: 'Shanyang's southern Pingyang*'—text breaks off.
translations.idiomatic: p. 1433, l. 10: lacuna after southern Pingyang.

translations.literal: (Gao)
translations.idiomatic: Place-name syllable Gao (稾) for the fragment below.

sentences.zh: **[橐]*湖陵五縣 據殿本考證及集解引沉欽韓說改。

translations.literal: **[Tuo]*Huling—five counties'—according to the palace edition's textual notes and the Jijie citing Shen Qinhan, emended.
translations.idiomatic: Emended per palace Kaozheng and Shen Qinhan (Jijie) to **[Tuo]* and Huling, five counties.'

sentences.zh: 注同。

translations.literal: The commentary agrees.
translations.idiomatic: The note matches this reading.

sentences.zh: 一四三五頁一四行 宦三年矣 按：「三」原斗「二」，逕改正。

translations.literal: Page 1435, line 14: 'has been a eunuch three years'—comment: 三 was originally corrupted to 二; corrected outright.
translations.idiomatic: p. 1435, l. 14: restore 三 (three); editions wrongly had 二 (two).

sentences.zh: 一四三五頁一四行 而為簞食*[與肉以]*與之 據汲本、殿本補。

translations.literal: Page 1435, line 14: 'yet made him a basket of food *[and meat to]* give him'—supplied according to the Ji and palace editions.
translations.idiomatic: p. 1435, l. 14: Ji and palace editions supply '*and meat to*' in the basket-of-rice clause.

sentences.zh: 一四三五頁一四行 既而*

translations.literal: Page 1435, line 14: 'thereupon*'—text breaks off.
translations.idiomatic: p. 1435, l. 14: lacuna after 'thereupon.'

translations.literal: (and)
translations.idiomatic: Particle gloss 與 linking the broken clauses.

sentences.zh: **[輒]*為公介*[士]* 據汲本、殿本刪補。

translations.literal: **[then]* became ducal escort *[gentleman]*'—deleted and supplied according to the Ji and palace editions.
translations.idiomatic: Ji and palace editions delete/supplement to **then* as ducal guard *gentleman*.'

sentences.zh: 一四三七頁四行 快然意解 按：校補引錢大昭說，謂「快」通鑒作「恢」，注云恢然猶廓然也。

translations.literal: Page 1437, line 4: 'pleased, his mind released'—comment: the Jiaobu cites Qian Dazhao, who says 快 in the Comprehensive Mirror is 恢; the note says 恢然 is like 廓然 (broadly open).
translations.idiomatic: p. 1437, l. 4: Zizhi tongjian reads 恢 for 快—annotation glosses it as an expansive relief.

sentences.zh: 一四三九頁一四行 *[禮云]*伯父歸寧乃國 據汲本補。

translations.literal: Page 1439, line 14: '*[The rites say]* the elder paternal uncle returns to comfort the state'—supplied according to the Ji edition.
translations.idiomatic: p. 1439, l. 14: Ji edition adds the lead-in '*Rites say*' before the uncle's homecoming line.

sentences.zh: 按：殿本作「禮伯父歸寧乃國」。

translations.literal: Comment: the palace edition reads 'Rites: the elder paternal uncle returns to comfort the state.'
translations.idiomatic: Palace edition runs the line as 'Rites: the uncle's homecoming to the state.'

sentences.zh: 刊誤謂此語本出儀禮，既下文有「詩云」字，既此亦當有「禮云」字。

translations.literal: The Kanwu says this phrase comes from the Yili; since below there is the phrase 'the Odes say,' here too there ought to be 'the Rites say.'
translations.idiomatic: Kanwu: the line comes from Yili; because 'the Odes say' appears later, 'the Rites say' should lead this clause.

sentences.zh: 一四四〇頁四行 乃獨封蒼五女為縣公主 按：袁紀云封女三人皆為公主。

translations.literal: Page 1440, line 4: 'then uniquely enfeoffed Cang's five daughters as county princesses'—comment: the Yuan ji says three daughters were all enfeoffed as princesses.
translations.idiomatic: p. 1440, l. 4: Yuan ji records three princesses, not five.

sentences.zh: 一四四一頁三行 輿馬 按：校補引柳從辰說，謂東觀記作「賾馬」。

translations.literal: Page 1441, line 3: 'carriages and horses'—comment: the Jiaobu cites Liu Congchen, who says the Eastern Lodge Record reads 賾馬.
translations.idiomatic: p. 1441, l. 3: Dongguan Han ji has a different graph cluster for 'horses' (賾馬).

sentences.zh: 一四四一頁九行 惟建初八年三月己卯 按：校補引錢大昭說，謂紀作「辛卯」。

translations.literal: Page 1441, line 9: 'only Jianchu 8, third month, day jimao'—comment: the Jiaobu cites Qian Dazhao, who says the annals read xinmao.
translations.idiomatic: p. 1441, l. 9: Annals give xinmao instead of jimao for that date.

sentences.zh: 一四四二頁四行 忠立*

translations.literal: Page 1442, line 4: 'Zhong established*'—text breaks off.
translations.idiomatic: p. 1442, l. 4: lacuna after 'Zhong ruled.'

translations.literal: (ten)
translations.idiomatic: Superfluous graph 十 (ten) before 'one year' in the received text.

sentences.zh: *一年薨 集解引洪頤粻說，謂憲王建初八年薨，忠即以是年嗣，章帝紀元和元年九月乙未東平王忠薨，忠立僅一年，「十」字衍。

translations.literal: *one year then died'—the Jijie cites Hong Yisun, who says King Xian died Jianchu 8 and Zhong succeeded that same year; the annals of Emperor Zhang, Yuanhe 1, ninth month, day yiwei, record King Zhong of Dongping died—Zhong had been established only one year—the character 十 is redundant.
translations.idiomatic: Hong Yisun (Jijie): King Xian died in Jianchu 8; Zhong succeeded and died in Yuanhe 1 after one year—delete stray 十.

sentences.zh: 今據刪。

translations.literal: Now according to this it is deleted.
translations.idiomatic: The erroneous 十 is removed accordingly.

sentences.zh: 一四四二頁六行 驃騎時吏 殿本考證謂「時」字應從通鑒作「府」。

translations.literal: Page 1442, line 6: 'cavalry attendant clerk'—the palace Kaozheng says the character 時 should follow the Comprehensive Mirror as 府 (office).
translations.idiomatic: p. 1442, l. 6: Palace editors prefer 府 (office) as in Zizhi tongjian for 時.

sentences.zh: 今按：此謂蒼為驃騎將軍時之掾屬，「時」字亦非鬥，特通鑒改云「府吏」，較為明確耳。

translations.literal: Commentator's note: this means retainers when Cang was general of agile cavalry; the character 時 is not a corruption—it is only that the Comprehensive Mirror altered it to 'office clerk,' which is somewhat clearer.
translations.idiomatic: These were staff when Cang held the cavalry command—時 is sound; Tongjian's 府 clerk is just clearer English.

sentences.zh: 一四四三頁七行 左傳*

translations.literal: Page 1443, line 7: 'Zuo Commentary*'—text breaks off.
translations.idiomatic: p. 1443, l. 7: lacuna after Zuo zhuan.

translations.literal: (says)
translations.idiomatic: Superfluous 曰—supplied in error before the quotation.

sentences.zh: *晉大夫士蒍之辭也 「曰」字衍，各本皆未正，今刪。

translations.literal: *words of Jin grandee Shi Wei'—the character 曰 is redundant; no edition has corrected it; now deleted.
translations.idiomatic: Continues the lacuna: the line attributes a saying to Jin minister Shi Wei. The stray 曰 is editorial noise and is struck.

sentences.zh: 一四四四頁一行 桓帝立河閒孝王子*

translations.literal: Page 1444, line 1: 'Emperor Huan established the Filial King of Hejian's son*'
translations.idiomatic: p. 1444, l. 1: lacuna after the king of Hejian's son.

sentences.zh: (恭為)

translations.literal: (Gong wei)
translations.idiomatic: Erroneous gloss 'Gong wei'—see following collation.

sentences.zh: *參戶亭侯博為任城王 刊誤謂當作「桓帝立河閒孝王恭子參戶亭侯博為任城王」。

translations.literal: *Marquis of Canhu pavilion Bo as king of Rencheng'—the Kanwu says it should read 'Emperor Huan established the Filial King of Hejian Gong's son, marquis of Canhu pavilion Bo, as king of Rencheng.'
translations.idiomatic: Kanwu: restore full line naming Gong's son Bo, marquis of Canhu, as king of Rencheng.

sentences.zh: 校補謂河閒孝王名開，不名恭，且謚以易名，諸王既稱謚，即不必定著其名，「恭為」二字皆屬誤衍。

translations.literal: The Jiaobu says the Filial King of Hejian was named Kai, not Gong; moreover posthumous titles replace personal names—once kings bear their posthumous epithets their personal names need not be fixed, so both characters 恭為 are erroneous interpolation.
translations.idiomatic: Jiaobu: the prince was Kai; 'Gong wei' is spurious once the temple name is used.

sentences.zh: 今據校補說刪。

translations.literal: Now according to the Jiaobu explanation it is deleted.
translations.idiomatic: Deleted per Jiaobu.

sentences.zh: 一四四四頁四行 靈帝復立河閒貞王*

translations.literal: Page 1444, line 4: 'Emperor Ling again established the Upright King of Hejian*'
translations.idiomatic: p. 1444, l. 4: lacuna after the Upright King of Hejian.

translations.literal: (Xun)
translations.idiomatic: Wrong graph Xun for Jian—see note.

translations.literal: **[Jian]**[zi]* marquis of Xinchang*'
translations.idiomatic: Broken text: should name Jian's son, marquis of Xinchang.

translations.literal: (zi)
translations.idiomatic: Particle zi showing misplaced word order in editions.

sentences.zh: *佗為任城王 校補謂貞王名建，靈帝紀及河閒孝王傳皆同，此作「遜」，誤。

translations.literal: *Tuo as king of Rencheng'—the Jiaobu says the Upright King was named Jian—the Lingdi ji and Hejian Filial King biography agree—here writing 遜 is wrong.
translations.idiomatic: Jiaobu: Upright King's name is Jian; 遜 is error.

sentences.zh: 又汲本、殿本「子」字在「新昌侯」上。

translations.literal: Further, in the Ji edition and palace edition the character 子 stands above 'marquis of Xinchang.'
translations.idiomatic: Ji and palace editions move 子 before Xinchang marquis.

sentences.zh: 今據改。

translations.literal: Now according to this it is emended.
translations.idiomatic: Emended accordingly.

sentences.zh: 一四四七頁一六行 *

translations.literal: Page 1447, line 16: '*'
translations.idiomatic: p. 1447, l. 16: lacuna.

translations.literal: (Hong)
translations.idiomatic: Marginal syllable Hong—corrupt for Hong in Hongfan.

sentences.zh: **[洪]*范五行傳 據汲本、殿本改。

translations.literal: **[Hong]*fan treatise on the Five Phases'—according to the Ji and palace editions, emended.
translations.idiomatic: Emended to Hongfan per Ji and palace texts.

sentences.zh: 一四四九頁四行 封左*

translations.literal: Page 1449, line 4: 'enfeoffed as left*'
translations.idiomatic: p. 1449, l. 4: lacuna after left.

translations.literal: (Feng)
translations.idiomatic: Spurious Feng gloss—same issue as Right Yi.

sentences.zh: *翊公 刊誤謂案光武紀封焉左翊公，與右翊相配。

translations.literal: *Yi duke'—the Kanwu says checking the Guangwu annals enfeoffs Yan as duke of Left Yi, pairing with Right Yi.
translations.idiomatic: Kanwu: Guangwu ji pairs Left and Right Yi.

sentences.zh: 今按：此衍「馮」字，今刪，參閱前「封右翊公」條校記。

translations.literal: Commentator's note: here 馮 is redundant—now deleted; see earlier collation on 'enfeoffed as duke of Right Yi.'
translations.idiomatic: Drop redundant 馮; cf. Right Yi note.

sentences.zh: 一四四九頁五行 諸王來會辟雍 按：「辟」原斗「璧」，逕據汲本、殿本改正。

translations.literal: Page 1449, line 5: 'the kings came to gather at the Ring Moat Hall'—comment: 辟 was corrupted to 璧; corrected per Ji and palace editions.
translations.idiomatic: p. 1449, l. 5: restore 辟 for Biyong; was miswritten 璧.

sentences.zh: 一四五〇頁九行 爾雅曰女子之子為出也 汲本、殿本「為」作「謂」。

translations.literal: Page 1450, line 9: 'the Erya says a woman's child is called chu'—the Ji and palace editions read 謂 for 為.
translations.idiomatic: p. 1450, l. 9: Ji/palace use 謂 (calls) for 為.

sentences.zh: 按：爾雅云「男子謂姊妹之子為出」。

translations.literal: Comment: the Erya states 'a man calls his sister's son chu.'
translations.idiomatic: Erya line: a man applies chu to a sister's son.

sentences.zh: 虎賁、旄頭、鐘虡解見光武紀。

translations.literal: Tiger guard, plume vanguard, bell racks—explanations appear in the Guangwu annals.
translations.idiomatic: See Guangwu ji for tiger guard, mane vanguard, bells.

sentences.zh: 縣音玄。

translations.literal: County is pronounced xuan.
translations.idiomatic: Read 縣 as xuan (here).

sentences.zh: 恭王名余，景帝之子。

translations.literal: King Gong was named Yu; he was a son of Emperor Jing.
translations.idiomatic: King Gong of Lu—Yu—was Emperor Jing's son.

sentences.zh: 殿在今兗州曲阜城中，故基東西二十丈，南北十二丈，高丈餘也。

translations.literal: The hall stands today inside Qufu city in Yan prefecture; the old foundations measure twenty east-west by twelve south-north bu, over one zhang high.
translations.idiomatic: The ruin sits in Qufu: roughly twenty by twelve bu, over one zhang tall.

sentences.zh: 浸，漸也。

translations.literal: Jin means gradually.
translations.idiomatic: Jin: 'gradually,' 'by degrees.'

sentences.zh: 杜預注左傳云：「地中之泉，故曰黃泉。」

translations.literal: Du Yu's note on the Zuo Commentary says: 'The spring beneath the earth—therefore it is called Yellow Spring.'
translations.idiomatic: Du Yu: subterranean spring = Yellow Spring.

sentences.zh: 無男，無多男也。

translations.literal: No male means not many males.
translations.idiomatic: 'No male heir' means lacking abundant sons.

sentences.zh: 即婦人封侯也，若呂后之妹呂須封為臨光侯，蕭何夫人封為酇侯之類。

translations.literal: That is, women enfeoffed as marquises—such as Empress Lü's younger sister Lü Xu enfeoffed as marquis of Lingguang, or Xiao He's wife enfeoffed as marquis of Zan.
translations.idiomatic: Examples of women as marquises: Lü Xu, Xiao He's wife.

sentences.zh: 私計天恩，不敢忘也。

translations.literal: In private reckoning heaven's grace—dare not forget.
translations.idiomatic: Privately I reckon imperial grace and dare not forget it.

sentences.zh: 光武崩也。

translations.literal: Guangwu died.
translations.idiomatic: Refers to Guangwu's death.

sentences.zh: 津門，洛陽南面西頭門也，一名津陽門。

translations.literal: Jin Gate is the westernmost gate on Luoyang's south face; it is also called Jinyang Gate.
translations.idiomatic: Jinmen: southwest gate of Luoyang, also Jinyang.

sentences.zh: 每門皆有亭。

translations.literal: Each gate had a pavilion.
translations.idiomatic: Every city gate had its pavilion.

sentences.zh: 解並見光武及明帝紀。

translations.literal: Explanations together appear in the Guangwu and Mingdi annals.
translations.idiomatic: Cross-ref. Guangwu and Mingdi ji.

sentences.zh: 四姓小侯，解見明帝紀。

translations.literal: The four-surname minor marquises—explanation appears in the Mingdi annals.
translations.idiomatic: Four-surname minor hou—see Mingdi ji.

sentences.zh: 夫人，蓋小侯之母也。

translations.literal: Consort—presumably the minor marquis's mother.
translations.idiomatic: Lady means the young marquis's mother.

sentences.zh: 前書曰：「卓獸不腢者，河閒獻王近之矣。」

translations.literal: The Former Han says: 'As for one outstanding and not herd-like, King Xian of Hejian came near that.'
translations.idiomatic: Hanshu: King Xian of Hejian neared the ideal of standing apart from the crowd.

sentences.zh: 眥或為瘠。

translations.literal: Zi may read ji (emaciated).
translations.idiomatic: Variant: 眥 or 瘠.

sentences.zh: 既祥之後而服練也。

translations.literal: After the auspicious rites then wearing fine-weave mourning.
translations.idiomatic: After xiang rites, wear lian (fine hemp).

sentences.zh: 禮記曰：「練衣黃裡縓緣。

translations.literal: The Record of Rites says: 'Fine-weave upper garment with yellow lining and quan selvage.
translations.idiomatic: Liji quotation (opening).

sentences.zh: 」縓即紅也。

translations.literal: ' Quan is red.
translations.idiomatic: Quan means reddish trim.

sentences.zh: 縓音七絹反。

translations.literal: Quan is read quan, seventh entering tone + juan reverse.
translations.idiomatic: Quan: fanqie reading noted.

sentences.zh: 鄭玄注周禮曰：「淺絳也。」

translations.literal: Zheng Xuan's note on the Zhou li says: 'Light crimson.'
translations.idiomatic: Zheng Xuan: light crimson.

sentences.zh: 左氏傳曰：「晏桓子卒，晏嬰麤衰斬，苴絰帶，杖，菅屨，食粥，居倚廬，寢苫枕草。

translations.literal: The Zuo Tradition says: 'When Huanzi of Yan died, Yan Ying wore coarse sackcloth with torn edges, hemp belt and staff, rush sandals, ate gruel, dwelt in a leaning hut, slept on straw and pillowed rush.
translations.idiomatic: Zuo: Yan Ying's mourning for Huanzi.

sentences.zh: 其家老曰：『非大夫之禮也。

translations.literal: His household elder said: 'That is not the rite for a grandee.'
translations.idiomatic: House elder objects.

sentences.zh: 』」杜預注云：「時士及大夫衰服各有不同。」

translations.literal: ' Du Yu's note says: 'At that time gentlemen and grandees differed in mourning dress.'
translations.idiomatic: Du Yu: ranks differed in mourning.

sentences.zh: 詩周頌之文。

translations.literal: Text from the Zhou song of the Odes.
translations.idiomatic: From Zhou song.

sentences.zh: 克，能也。

translations.literal: Ke means able.
translations.idiomatic: Ke: 'able to.'

sentences.zh: 僮，縣，屬臨淮郡，故城在今泗州宿預縣西南。

translations.literal: Tong—county—belonged to Linhuai commandery; the old town lies southwest of modern Suyu county, Si prefecture.
translations.idiomatic: Tong county, Linhuai—site southwest of Suyu.

sentences.zh: 釐音僖，下皆同。

translations.literal: Xi is read xi; below all the same.
translations.idiomatic: Xi pronunciation; same below.

sentences.zh: 取慮，縣，故城在今泗州下邳縣西南。

translations.literal: Qulü—county—old town southwest of Xiapi county, Si prefecture.
translations.idiomatic: Qulü county—southwest of Xiapi.

sentences.zh: 案：臨淮無須昌，有昌陽縣，蓋誤也。

translations.literal: Comment: Linhuai had no Xuchang; it had Changyang county—probably error.
translations.idiomatic: Linhuai lacked Xuchang—likely mistook Changyang.

sentences.zh: 取慮音秋閭。

translations.literal: Qulü is pronounced qiulü.
translations.idiomatic: Qulü read qiulü.

sentences.zh: 龍舒，縣，屬廬江郡，故城在今廬州廬江縣西也。

translations.literal: Longshu—county—belonged to Lujiang commandery; old town west of Lujiang county, Lu prefecture today.
translations.idiomatic: Longshu in Lujiang—west of modern Lujiang.

sentences.zh: 袁宏漢紀：「浮屠，佛也，西域天竺國有佛道焉。

translations.literal: Yuan Hong's Han ji: 'Fututu—that is Buddha; in the western regions the kingdom Tianzhu has the Buddha path.
translations.idiomatic: Yuan Hong: Fututu is Buddha; Tianzhu had the Buddhist teaching.

sentences.zh: 佛者，漢言覺也，將以覺悟腢生也。

translations.literal: As for Buddha, in Han parlance it means awakening; he would awaken the myriad-born.
translations.idiomatic: 'Buddha' glosses as awakening—to awaken sentient beings.

sentences.zh: 其教以修善慈心為主，不殺生，專務清靜。

translations.literal: Their doctrine focused on cultivating goodness and a compassionate mind; they did not kill living things and devoted themselves wholly to purity and stillness.
translations.idiomatic: The teaching stressed kindness and compassion, ahimsa, and quietist practice.

sentences.zh: 其精者為沙門。

translations.literal: Those refined became śramaṇas.
translations.idiomatic: Elite practitioners became śramaṇas.

sentences.zh: 沙門，漢言息也，蓋息意去欲而歸於無為。

translations.literal: Śramaṇa, in Han parlance means rest—presumably resting intent and discarding desire to return to non-action.
translations.idiomatic: Śramaṇa glossed as 'rest'—still the mind and cease craving.

sentences.zh: 又以為人死精神不滅，隨復受形，生時善惡皆有報應，故貴行善修道，以煉精神，以至無生而得為佛也。

translations.literal: They also held that when people died their spirits did not perish but took form again; good and evil in life each received retribution—therefore they prized doing good and cultivating the path to refine the spirit until reaching non-birth and thereby attaining buddhahood.
translations.idiomatic: They taught rebirth, karmic reward, discipline toward awakening.

sentences.zh: 佛長丈六尺，黃金色，項中佩日月光，變化無方，無所不入，而大濟腢生。

translations.literal: The Buddha stood sixteen chi tall, golden in color; around his neck hung sun and moon light; his transformations were limitless—there was nowhere he could not enter—and he broadly delivered the myriad-born.
translations.idiomatic: Iconography: sixteen-foot golden body, radiant neck, omnipresent savior.

sentences.zh: 初，明帝夢見金人長大，項有日月光，以問腢臣。

translations.literal: At first Emperor Ming dreamed of a golden man, tall, with sun and moon about his neck; he asked his ministers.
translations.idiomatic: Ming's dream of the golden man.

sentences.zh: 或曰：『西方有神，其名曰佛。

translations.literal: Someone said: 'In the west there is a god; his name is Buddha.
translations.idiomatic: Courtier: a western deity named Buddha.

sentences.zh: 陛下所夢，得無是乎？

translations.literal: Could what Your Majesty dreamed be that?'
translations.idiomatic: Perhaps Your Majesty's dream was he?

sentences.zh: 』於是遣使天竺，問其道術而圖其形像焉。」

translations.literal: ' Thereupon he dispatched envoys to Tianzhu to inquire into its techniques and to depict its likeness.'
translations.idiomatic: Envoys sent to India for doctrine and images.

sentences.zh: 伊蒲塞即優婆塞也，中華翻為近住，言受戒行堪近僧住也。

translations.literal: Yipusai is upāsaka; in Chinese it is rendered 'near dweller,' meaning having received precepts and conduct fit to dwell near monks.
translations.idiomatic: Upāsaka = lay follower staying near the sangha.

sentences.zh: 桑門即沙門。

translations.literal: Sangmen is śramaṇa.
translations.idiomatic: Sangmen means śramaṇa.

sentences.zh: 今宣州縣也。

translations.literal: It is Xuancheng county today.
translations.idiomatic: Modern Xuancheng county.

sentences.zh: 湯沐，解見皇后紀也。

translations.literal: Tang-mu—explanation appears in the empress biography.
translations.idiomatic: Tangmu bath towns—see empress treatise.

sentences.zh: 軿猶屏也，自隱蔽之車。

translations.literal: Ping is like ping—a self-screening carriage.
translations.idiomatic: Ping carriage: enclosed, screened.

sentences.zh: 蒼頡篇曰：「衣車也。」

translations.literal: The Cangjie pian says: 'A clothing carriage.'
translations.idiomatic: Cangjie pian: curtained carriage.

sentences.zh: 占護猶守護也。

translations.literal: Zhanhu means the same as guard and protect.
translations.idiomatic: Zhanhu = to guard.

sentences.zh: 續漢輿服志曰：「諸侯王赤綬四采，長二丈一尺。

translations.literal: The Xu Han Vehicle and Garb Treatise says: 'Princes of blood wear red ribbons of four hues, two zhang one chi long.
translations.idiomatic: Xu Han yufu: royal kin red sash.

sentences.zh: 皇子安車，青蓋金華藻。」

translations.literal: An imperial son's secure carriage—green canopy with golden flowering ornament.'
translations.idiomatic: Heir's carriage: green canopy, gold ornament.

sentences.zh: 六，縣名，屬廬江郡。

translations.literal: Liu—county name—belonged to Lujiang commandery.
translations.idiomatic: Liu county, Lujiang.

sentences.zh: 東武城屬清河郡，今貝州武城縣是。

translations.literal: Dongwucheng belonged to Qinghe commandery; it is modern Wu county in Bei prefecture.
translations.idiomatic: Dongwucheng = Bei Zhou's Wucheng.

sentences.zh: 東朝陽在今齊州臨濟縣東。

translations.literal: East Chaoyang lies east of Linji county in Qi prefecture today.
translations.idiomatic: East Chaoyang east of Linji.

sentences.zh: 西平昌，今德州般縣也。

translations.literal: West Pingchang is modern Pan county in De prefecture.
translations.idiomatic: West Pingchang = Dezhou Pan.

sentences.zh: 般音補滿反。

translations.literal: Pan is read pu + man reverse.
translations.idiomatic: Pan: fanqie pu-man.

sentences.zh: 孝經諸侯章之義也。

translations.literal: This follows the sense of the Feudal Lords chapter of the Classic of Filial Piety.
translations.idiomatic: Cf. Xiaojing, feudal lords chapter.

sentences.zh: 臺、隸賤職也，左氏傳曰：「人有十等，王臣公，公臣卿，卿臣大夫，大夫臣士，士臣皁，皁臣輿，輿臣隸，隸臣僚，僚臣僕，僕臣臺」也。

translations.literal: Tai and li are lowly offices; the Zuo Tradition says: 'People have ten ranks: the king's ministers are dukes; dukes minister to ministers; ministers to grandees; grandees to gentlemen; gentlemen to black-robes; black-robes to bearers; bearers to li; li to petty officers; petty officers to servants; servants to tai.'
translations.idiomatic: The note quotes Zuo's ten-rung ladder of service: at the bottom stand menial posts such as li and tai, the meanest of the mean.

sentences.zh: 言如蠶之食，漸至衰盡也。

translations.literal: It says like silkworms feeding—gradually gnawing away to exhaustion.
translations.idiomatic: Like silkworms eating silk—slow ruin.

sentences.zh: 巨，大也。

translations.literal: Ju means great.
translations.idiomatic: Ju: 'great.'

sentences.zh: 大萬謂萬萬。

translations.literal: Great wan means myriad myriads.
translations.idiomatic: Da wan = ten thousand times ten thousand.

sentences.zh: 荒，廢也。

translations.literal: Huang means waste.
translations.idiomatic: Huang: waste, neglect.

sentences.zh: 文彩繁多，則質以之廢，土木增構，則人殫其力，故云人亡。

translations.literal: When patterned splendor grows excessive, substance is wasted by it; when earthworks multiply, people exhaust their strength—therefore it says people perish.
translations.idiomatic: Ornament wastes substance; megaprojects exhaust the people.

sentences.zh: 左氏傳，楚靈王成章華之臺，後卒被殺。

translations.literal: The Zuo Tradition: King Ling of Chu built the Zhanghua terrace and was later killed.
translations.idiomatic: Chu Lingwang's Zhanghua terrace—then assassinated.

sentences.zh: 杜預注云「臺在今南郡華容縣」也。

translations.literal: Du Yu's note says 'the terrace is today in Huarong county, Nan commandery.'
translations.idiomatic: Du Yu locates terrace in Huarong.

sentences.zh: 姑蘇臺一名姑胥臺。

translations.literal: The Gusu terrace is also called the Guxu terrace.
translations.idiomatic: Gusu / Guxu terrace.

sentences.zh: 越絕書曰：「胥門外有九曲路，闔廬以游姑蘇之臺，以望湖中。

translations.literal: The Yue jue shu says: 'Outside Xu Gate is a nine-bend road; Helü toured the Gusu terrace to gaze on the lake.
translations.idiomatic: Yue jue shu: Helü at Gusu.

sentences.zh: 」顧夷*

translations.literal: ' Gu Yi*
translations.idiomatic: Break after Gu Yi.

translations.literal: (Wu)
translations.idiomatic: Wu gloss for next line.

sentences.zh: **[吳]*地記云：「橫山北有小山，俗謂姑蘇臺。

translations.literal: **[Wu]*di ji says: 'North of Heng Mountain is a small hill; folk call it the Gusu terrace.
translations.idiomatic: Wu di ji: small hill north of Hengshan.

sentences.zh: 」在今蘇州吳縣西。

translations.literal: ' It is west of Wu county, Suzhou today.
translations.idiomatic: West of Suzhou's Wu county.

sentences.zh: 闔廬後被越殺之。

translations.literal: Later Helü was killed by Yue.
translations.idiomatic: Helü later killed by Yue.

sentences.zh: 論語：「齊景公有馬千駟，死之日，人無德而稱焉。

translations.literal: The Analects: 'Duke Jing of Qi had a thousand teams of horses; the day he died, people found no virtue to praise in him.
translations.idiomatic: Analects: Jing's thousand teams, no virtue.

sentences.zh: 」千駟，四千匹。

translations.literal: ' A thousand teams is four thousand horses.
translations.idiomatic: One team = four horses; thousand teams = four thousand.

sentences.zh: 錯音七故反。

translations.literal: Cuo is read cuo, seventh entering + gu reverse.
translations.idiomatic: Cuo: fanqie.

sentences.zh: 四府掾史皆無四十人，今特置以優之也。

translations.literal: In the four bureaus clerks were never forty persons—here they specially established that number to honor him.
translations.idiomatic: Four offices usually under forty clerks—exception made as honor.

sentences.zh: 南平陽，縣，故城今兗州鄒縣也。

translations.literal: South Pingyang—county—the old town is Zou county in Yan prefecture today.
translations.idiomatic: South Pingyang lay where modern Zou county, Yanzhou, stands.

translations.literal: An asterisk marks a lacuna between place-names in the commentary line.
translations.idiomatic: The lone asterisk flags missing text in the manuscript.

translations.literal: (Gao)
translations.idiomatic: The syllable Gao (稾) links to the place-name below.

sentences.zh: **[橐]*，縣，一名高平，故城在鄒縣西南。

translations.literal: **[Tuo]*—county—also called Gaoping; old town southwest of Zou county.
translations.idiomatic: Tuo/Gao county southwest of Zou—also named Gaoping.

sentences.zh: 湖陵故城在今兗州防與縣東南。

translations.literal: Huling's old town lies southeast of Fangyu county in Yan prefecture today.
translations.idiomatic: Ancient Huling southeast of present Fangyu.

sentences.zh: 其志今亡。

translations.literal: That treatise is lost today.
translations.idiomatic: That gazetteer no longer survives.

sentences.zh: 第，宅也。

translations.literal: Di means a residence.
translations.idiomatic: Di: a ranked mansion.

sentences.zh: 有甲乙之次，故曰第。

translations.literal: There is ordering from first to last—hence called di.
translations.idiomatic: Houses graded jia, yi, etc.—hence 'ranked estate.'

sentences.zh: 禮記月令曰「孟春之月，無聚大觿，無置城郭。

translations.literal: The Record of Rites monthly ordinance says: 'In the first month of spring do not assemble great fish-traps, do not raise city walls.'
translations.idiomatic: Yueling: early spring—no mass hunts or wall works.

sentences.zh: 仲春之月，無作大事，以防農事」也。

translations.literal: 'In the second month of spring do not launch great projects, lest you harm the farming season.'
translations.idiomatic: Mid-spring: forbid works that disrupt tillage.

sentences.zh: 尚書五行傳曰：「田獵不宿，飲食不享，出入不節，奪人農時，及有奸謀，則木不曲直。

translations.literal: The Document of History Five Phases treatise says: 'When hunting does not stay overnight, feasting lacks sacrifice, comings and goings lack restraint, farming seasons are seized, and treachery is abroad—then wood fails to bend and straighten.'
translations.idiomatic: Hongfan: wood element faults when hunts, feasts, timing, and plots go wrong.

sentences.zh: 」鄭玄注云：「木性或曲或直，人所用為器者也。

translations.literal: ' Zheng Xuan notes: 'Wood may bend or stand straight—people fashion it into tools.'
translations.idiomatic: Zheng Xuan on workable timber.

sentences.zh: 無故生不暢茂，多有折槁，是為不曲直也。

translations.literal: ' Without cause it does not flourish; much snaps and withers—this is failing to bend and straighten.'
translations.idiomatic: Distorted growth means the wood omen.

sentences.zh: 」前書音義曰：「不宿，不預戒日也。」

translations.literal: ' Former Han glosses say: 'Does not lodge overnight—does not fix the day in advance.'
translations.idiomatic: Yinyi: 'no overnight hunt' = no scheduled slaughter day.

sentences.zh: 皆游散之意。

translations.literal: All convey roaming and rambling.
translations.idiomatic: These glosses mean idle wandering.

sentences.zh: 詩曰：「於焉消搖。

translations.literal: The Odes say: 'There I wander and roam at ease.'
translations.idiomatic: Shijing: carefree wandering.

sentences.zh: 」左氏傳曰：「橫流而仿佯。

translations.literal: The Zuo Tradition says: 'Drifting cross-current and wandering.'
translations.idiomatic: Zuo: drifting at large.

sentences.zh: 」前書音義曰：「弭節猶按節也，言不盡意馳驅也。」

translations.literal: Former Han glosses say: 'Mijie is like checking the tally—not galloping without limit.'
translations.idiomatic: Yinyi: mijie reins in the pace.

sentences.zh: 旄謂注旄於竿首。

translations.literal: Mao means fixing yak-tail plumes atop the pole.
translations.idiomatic: Standard with yak hair at the staff head.

sentences.zh: 詩大雅之文也。

translations.literal: Text from the Great Elegantiae of the Odes.
translations.idiomatic: From Da ya.

sentences.zh: 抑抑，密也。

translations.literal: Yiyi means restrained and dense.
translations.idiomatic: Yiyi: taut, dignified bearing.

sentences.zh: 隅，廉也。

translations.literal: Yu means corner—lian.
translations.idiomatic: Yu: 'corner,' architectural edge.

sentences.zh: 言人審密於威儀抑抑然者，其德必嚴正，如宮室之制，內繩直則外有廉隅。

translations.literal: It says one who is careful and restrained in majestic bearing—his virtue must be stern and upright—like a hall's plan: inner cords straight then outer corners appear.
translations.idiomatic: Inner discipline yields outer dignity—like straight foundation lines.

sentences.zh: 負薪，喻小人也。

translations.literal: Bearing firewood figures the petty man.
translations.idiomatic: Woodcutter load = commoner imagery.

sentences.zh: 易曰：「負且乘，致寇至。

translations.literal: The Changes say: 'Bearing a load yet riding brings robbers.'
translations.idiomatic: Zhou yi: porter riding invites theft.

sentences.zh: 」負也者小人之事，乘也者君子之器，以小人而乘君子之器，則盜思奪之矣。

translations.literal: ' Bearing is the petty man's work; riding is the gentleman's equipage—a petty man mounted on a gentleman's carriage makes thieves covet it.'
translations.idiomatic: Menial work vs. noble carriage—mixed signals invite robbery.

sentences.zh: 簞，竹器也。

translations.literal: Dan is a bamboo vessel.
translations.idiomatic: Dan: bamboo food hamper.

sentences.zh: 圓曰簞，方曰笥。

translations.literal: Round is called dan; square is called si.
translations.idiomatic: Round basket dan; square box si.

sentences.zh: 左氏傳曰：「晉宣子田於首山，捨於翳桑，見靈輒餓，曰：『不食三日矣。

translations.literal: The Zuo Tradition says: 'Xuanzi of Jin hunted on Mount Shou, lodged at Yi mulberry, saw Ling Zhe starving, and said: You have not eaten three days.'
translations.idiomatic: Zuo: Zhao Dun meets starving Ling Zhe.

sentences.zh: 』食之，捨其半。

translations.literal: ' He fed him; Zhe set aside half.
translations.idiomatic: Zhe saves half for his mother.

sentences.zh: 問之，曰：『宦三年矣，未知母之存否，請遺之。

translations.literal: ' Asked, he said: I have been a house servant three years; I do not know if my mother lives—please let me send her this.'
translations.idiomatic: Zhe's plea for his mother.

sentences.zh: 』使盡之，而為簞食*〔與肉以〕*與之。

translations.literal: ' Ordered him to finish it, then prepared a basket of food *[with meat to]* give him.
translations.idiomatic: Dun adds meat to the rice bundle.

sentences.zh: 既而*

translations.literal: Thereupon*
translations.idiomatic: Lacuna after 'thereupon.'

translations.literal: (yu)
translations.idiomatic: Particle yu linking broken clauses.

sentences.zh: **〔輒〕*為公介*〔士〕*，倒戟以御公徒而免之。

translations.literal: **[Then]* as the duke's escort *[gentleman]*—reversed halberds to shield the duke's followers and save him.'
translations.idiomatic: Zhe repays the meal as Zhao's escort in ambush.

sentences.zh: 問何故，曰：『翳桑之餓人也。』」

translations.literal: Asked why—said: The starving man at Yi mulberry.'"
translations.idiomatic: He identifies himself as the mulberry beggar.

sentences.zh: 赤紱，大夫之服也。

translations.literal: Crimson sash cord—grandee's dress.
translations.idiomatic: Scarlet fu—minister's insignia.

sentences.zh: 詩曹風曰：「彼己之子，三百赤紱。

translations.literal: The Odes Cao feng says: 'Those noble men—three hundred crimson cords.'
translations.idiomatic: Cao feng satire on courtiers.

sentences.zh: 」刺其無德居位者多也。

translations.literal: ' It attacks how many without virtue hold office.
translations.idiomatic: Barbed portrait of overpaid nobles.

sentences.zh: 去王畿五百里曰甸服，又五百里曰侯服，又五百里曰綏服，又五百里要服，又五百里荒服。

translations.literal: Five hundred li from the royal domain is dian service; another five hundred hou; another five hundred sui; another five hundred yao; another five hundred huang.
translations.idiomatic: Five concentric zones at five hundred li each.

sentences.zh: 儆，備也，音警。

translations.literal: Jing means prepare; sounds like jing (alert).
translations.idiomatic: Read jing as 'prepare,' homophone with alarm.

sentences.zh: 有鼻，國名，其地在今永州營道縣北。

translations.literal: Youbi—state name—its ground lies north of Yingdao county in Yongzhou today.
translations.idiomatic: Youbi polity north of modern Yingdao.

sentences.zh: 史記曰舜弟象封於有鼻也。

translations.literal: The Records says Shun's younger brother Xiang was enfeoffed at Youbi.
translations.idiomatic: Shiji: Xiang's fief at Youbi.

sentences.zh: 漢官儀「將軍掾屬二十九人，中大夫無員，令史四十一人」也。

translations.literal: Han office regulations: 'A general's staff twenty-nine persons; senior counselors without quota; clerks forty-one persons.'
translations.idiomatic: Han guanyi staff ceilings.

sentences.zh: 采菽，詩小雅之章也。

translations.literal: Gathering beans—a chapter of the Odes Minor Elegantiae.
translations.idiomatic: Cai shu from Xiao ya.

sentences.zh: 其詩曰：「采菽采菽，筐之筥之，君子來朝，何錫與之？

translations.literal: Its poem says: 'Gather beans gather beans—in baskets and hampers—nobles come to court; what gifts for them?'
translations.idiomatic: Opening lines of Cai shu.

sentences.zh: 」毛萇注云：「菽所以芼大牢而待君子也。」

translations.literal: ' Mao Chang notes: 'Beans flavor the great victim awaiting nobles.'
translations.idiomatic: Mao: beans prep the royal banquet.

sentences.zh: 留禁中也。

translations.literal: Detained inside the palace.
translations.idiomatic: Kept within the inner court.

sentences.zh: 韋昭注國語曰：「有眸子而無見曰蒙。」

translations.literal: Wei Zhao's Guoyu gloss: 'Having pupils yet unable to see is called meng.'
translations.idiomatic: Wei Zhao defines blind meng.

sentences.zh: 詩國風也。

translations.literal: From the Airs of the Odes.
translations.idiomatic: Guo feng passage.

sentences.zh: 忡忡猶沖沖。

translations.literal: Chongchong is like chongchong (uneasy).
translations.idiomatic: Chongchong: restless, surging care.

sentences.zh: 降，下也。

translations.literal: Jiang means to descend—lower.
translations.idiomatic: Jiang: 'to lower,' 'set down.'

sentences.zh: 風俗通曰：「古姓，周有古公但父，其後氏焉。

translations.literal: Fengsu tong says: 'The ancient surname Gu—Zhou had elder lord Danfu—later generations took it as clan.'
translations.idiomatic: Gu surname from Danfu line.

sentences.zh: 」湼陽主，光武女，竇固之妻也。

translations.literal: Nieyang princess—Emperor Guangwu's daughter—wife of Dou Gu.
translations.idiomatic: Princess Nieyang married Dou Gu.

sentences.zh: 貫行謂一皆遵奉也。

translations.literal: Guanxing means uniformly obeyed.
translations.idiomatic: Guanxing: observe one thread throughout.

sentences.zh: 谷永曰「一以貫行，固執無違」也。

translations.literal: Gu Yong said 'bind them with one strand—cling without deviation.'
translations.idiomatic: Gu Yong's 'one cord of conduct.'

sentences.zh: 易曰：「謙德之柄。」

translations.literal: The Changes says: 'Humility is the handle of virtue.'
translations.idiomatic: Yi: humility as virtue's grip.

sentences.zh: 禮記曰：「古者墓而不墳。

translations.literal: The Record of Rites says: 'Antiquity buried in graves without mound heaps.'
translations.idiomatic: Liji: flat graves, no tumuli.

sentences.zh: 」故言不欲其著明。

translations.literal: ' Hence one says he did not want conspicuous display.
translations.idiomatic: Hence Guangwu avoided ostentatious tombs.

sentences.zh: 穀梁傳曰：「人之所聚曰都。

translations.literal: The Guliang Commentary says: 'Where people gather is called a capital.'
translations.idiomatic: Guliang: du means metropolis.

sentences.zh: 」杜預注左傳曰：「郛，郭也。」

translations.literal: Du Yu's Zuo Commentary gloss: 'Fu means outer wall.'
translations.idiomatic: Du Yu: fu is outer rampart.

sentences.zh: 五時衣謂春青，夏朱，季夏黃，秋白，冬黑也。

translations.literal: Five-seasons robes mean spring green, summer vermilion, late summer yellow, autumn white, winter black.
translations.idiomatic: Seasonal court colors.

sentences.zh: 衣單復具曰襲。

translations.literal: Garments layered lined and unlined together are called xi.
translations.idiomatic: Xi: layered winter/summer wear.

sentences.zh: 周禮：「追師掌王后之首服為副編。

translations.literal: Zhou li: 'The chief hairdresser manages the queen's headgear—fu and bian.'
translations.idiomatic: Zhou li on queen's hairpieces.

sentences.zh: 」鄭玄云：「副，婦人首服，三輔謂之假紒。

translations.literal: Zheng Xuan says: 'Fu is women's headgear—the Three Adjuncts call it false coils.'
translations.idiomatic: Zheng: ceremonial wig pieces.

sentences.zh: 」續漢書「帛」字作「皁」。

translations.literal: The Xu Hanshu writes bo where [some texts read] bo/black silk.
translations.idiomatic: Variant bo vs 皂 in Xu Hanshu.

sentences.zh: 詩國風曰：「凱風，美孝子也。

translations.literal: The Airs of the Odes say: 'Southwind praises filial sons.'
translations.idiomatic: Kai feng preface.

sentences.zh: 」「凱風自南，吹彼棘心，棘心夭夭，母氏劬勞。

translations.literal: ' Southwind from the south blows those jujube hearts—tender jujube hearts—mother labors sorely.'
translations.idiomatic: Lines from Kai feng.

sentences.zh: 爰有寒泉，在浚之下，有子七人，母氏勞苦。

translations.literal: There is cold spring below Jun—seven sons yet mother toils.
translations.idiomatic: Hanquan stanza continuation.

sentences.zh: 」寒泉在今濮州濮陽縣。

translations.literal: Cold spring lies today in Puyang county, Pu prefecture.
translations.idiomatic: Hanquan site at modern Puyang.

sentences.zh: 孔子廟在魯曲阜城中。

translations.literal: Confucius's temple stood inside Qufu city in Lu.
translations.idiomatic: Temple to Kongzi in Qufu.

sentences.zh: 伍緝之從征記曰：「魯人藏孔子所乘車於廟中，是顏路所請者也。

translations.literal: Wu Jizhi's Campaign Diary says: 'Lu people stored Confucius's carriage in the temple—the one Yan Lu requested.'
translations.idiomatic: Wu Jizhi on the saint's cart.

sentences.zh: 獻帝時，廟遇火，燒之。

translations.literal: During Emperor Xian the temple caught fire and burned it.
translations.idiomatic: Han Xiandi-era fire destroyed the relic.

sentences.zh: 」冠履解見鐘離意傳。

translations.literal: Caps and shoes—explanation appears in Zhongli Yi's biography.
translations.idiomatic: Footwear note—see Zhongli Yi.

sentences.zh: 前書天馬歌曰「太一況，天馬下，沾赤汗，沫流赭」也。

translations.literal: Former Han's Heavenly Horse song says 'Grand Unity gleams—heavenly horse descends—stained crimson sweat—froth flows ochre.'
translations.idiomatic: Tianma ge quotation.

sentences.zh: 閒音閒。

translations.literal: Jian is read jian.
translations.idiomatic: Read character jian as idle/leisure homophone.

sentences.zh: 說文曰：「貂，鼠屬也，大而黃黑，出丁零國。」

translations.literal: Shuowen says: 'Diao—a kind of rat—large yellow-black—comes from Dingling country.'
translations.idiomatic: Shuowen: sable-like diao from Dingling.

sentences.zh: 儀禮曰「覲禮，諸侯至於郊，王使皮弁用璧勞，侯氏亦皮弁迎於帷門之外，再拜。

translations.literal: The Yili says, 'In the audience rite, when the feudal lords reach the outer suburbs, the king sends messengers in leather caps with jade disks to condole; the visiting lord, also in leather cap, greets them outside the curtained gate, bowing twice.'
translations.idiomatic: The Yili scene: the lords halt at the outer sacrifice zone; the king's leather-capped heralds with jade reward them, and the guest lord, likewise capped, waits beyond the screen door and bows twice.

sentences.zh: 天子賜舍，曰：『賜伯父舍。

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven grants lodging, saying: 'We grant Uncle lodging.'
translations.idiomatic: Royal hospitality formula.

sentences.zh: 』同姓西面，北上；

translations.literal: Same surname faces west, ranked northward.
translations.idiomatic: Kin lords array west.

sentences.zh: 異姓東面，北上。

translations.literal: Different surname faces east, ranked northward.
translations.idiomatic: Alien lords face east.

sentences.zh: 侯氏裨冕，釋幣於檷，乘墨車，載龍旗、弧韣，乃朝以瑞玉，有繅。

translations.literal: The lord wears fu crown and mian, releases silk at the inner doorpost, rides black carriage bearing dragon banner and bow case—then audiences with jade tokens—cord attached.
